CuratorialText_x: From intimate courtyards to monumental temple, tomb, and pleasure gardens, Asia has been central to the development of cultivated landscapes. The earliest known garden—the biblical Garden of Eden—may have been located in West Asia at the confluence of the Tigris and the Euphrates in present-day Iraq. The very word “paradise” is derived from the walled orchard gardens and hunting parks of ancient Iran, referred to as pardis. This type of enclosed garden became an integral part of Persian architectural landscape for centuries and also often included pavilions to provide shade and relief from the sun. According to written sources, the earliest gardens in China, dating to the Zhou period (ca. 1050–256 BCE), consisted of enclosed hunting grounds reserved for the royal elite. Chinese gardens were often characterized by carefully positioned rocks and pools intended to recreate microcosms of nature at large. In Japan, gardens followed a more naturalistic design and incorporated rolling hills and languid ponds to underline harmony between humans and their surroundings. In the arid landscapes of West and South Asia, one of the most common garden plans depended on a series of interconnected pools and axial watercourses.

In the dry landscapes of West and South Asia, a common garden plan used a series of connected pools and straight water channels.

Largely coal powered, these machines spewed pollution that artists such as Whistler sought to visualize in paint.

CuratorialText_x: While recycling may seem like a very recent phenomenon, materials have long been reused in the creation of works of art, whether due to the scarcity of materials or to the value placed on an existing object. For example, since jade was such a prestigious medium and a valuable commodity in ancient China, broken or out-of-fashion articles were not discarded but were instead recycled to make new objects. This appears to be especially true in the late Shang dynasty (ca. 1250–1050 BCE). Archaeologists working at the late Shang capital of Anyang have found that, possibly due to the scarcity of resources at a time of high demand, earlier Stone Age (ca. 5000–1700 BCE) objects were being transformed into decorated jewelry and other items that held more interest and meaning to the Shang people. All three pendants shown here were made by cutting up a special kind of Neolithic square tube called a cong, which had lost its ritual significance by Shang times. It is noteworthy that the recycled cong used here represent different Stone Age cultures dispersed across north and south China.

Archaeologists working at the Shang capital, Anyang, found something interesting. They think that because resources were scarce and demand was high, people took objects from the Stone Age (about 5000–1700 BCE) and turned them into decorated jewelry and other items. These new objects had more meaning for the Shang people.

The three pendants shown here were all made from a special type of Neolithic square tube called a *cong*. By Shang times, the *cong* had lost its original ritual meaning. So people cut it up to make these pendants. It is worth noting that the recycled *cong* used here came from different Stone Age cultures across north and south China.

CuratorialText_x: Environmental conditions can significantly impact the preservation of works of art. Factors such as temperature, humidity, light, and pollution affect the chemical and physical properties of materials in different ways, causing damage and degradation to objects over time. On a larger scale, major environmental changes such as temperature shifts and more destructive weather patterns can threaten heritage sites around the world. Conservators play a pivotal role in safeguarding the objects in the museum’s collections. Read about conservation and scientific research initiatives at the National Museum of Asian Art. The Smithsonian Institution acknowledges climate change as the existential threat of our time. Learn more about the Smithsonian’s sustainability initiatives.

CuratorialText_x: Plants and animals can also be sources of reds used in artworks. Madder root and safflower each may have their color extracted, purified, and prepared for use in objects such as textiles and prints. The conditions in which the dye or lake is prepared can change the final color. Scale insects (such as lac and cochineal) are another source. Such insects were harvested, with the red color extracted and prepared for use as a pigment or dye. When possible, determining the colorants used in artworks can shed light on historical trade routes due to the limited native habitat of these insects.

CuratorialText_x: Rubrication, the practice of writing portions of a text in red ink, helped visually underscore certain information and became particularly common in European manuscripts after the seventh century. Red was sometimes used for the angular Kufic script in in copies of the Qur’an made between the eighth and eleventh centuries CE to indicate vocalization; it stood out next to black- or brown-inked calligraphy. Red was also commonly used in the paste for the inked seals that were added to finished works of Chinese painting or calligraphy; it contrasted against black ink writing and images. The seal might give the name of the artist or his studio, the collector, or other private individuals, and it occasionally expresses a favorite brief saying.

persona3_adapted: In many historical manuscripts, scribes used red ink to highlight important parts of the text—a practice known as rubrication. This technique became especially common in European books after the seventh century. In copies of the Qur’an made between the eighth and eleventh centuries, red was sometimes used for a specific angular style of writing called Kufic script, to show how words should be pronounced. The red stood out clearly against the black or brown ink used for the main calligraphy. Red was also commonly used in the paste for the inked seals that were added to finished Chinese paintings or calligraphy. These seals created a visual contrast with the black ink of the writing or images. A seal might include the name of the artist or their studio, the collector, or other private individuals, and occasionally it would express a favorite short saying.

In China, red is auspicious—associated with life-generating energy (the sun, blood, and fire)—and is the color of celebrations and prosperity.

CuratorialText_x: Red can carry more negative associations as well, such as with warfare, rage, and destruction. The color of blood, there is a visceral quality to red that resonates uniquely with the human psyche. Haunted by the traces that the human body leaves behind, contemporary artist Chiharu Shiota’s 2015 installation “Over the Continents” amassed personal memories through an accumulation of nearly four hundred individual shoes. Collected by the artist, each shoe came with a note from the donor describing lost individuals and past moments. Almost four miles of red yarn wrapped around the shoes and attached to a single point in the museum’s Sackler pavilion, evoking the artist’s attention to the fundamental ties that connect humans to one another and the world.

Explore this object in 3D.

CuratorialText_x: “Celadon” refers to a large family of high-fired ceramics. Their glazes are colored by small percentages of iron oxide. When fired in an oxygen-deprived, or reducing, atmosphere, the glazes turn variable shades of bluish green, traditionally described with the French word céladon. The word is commonly believed to have been derived from a seventeenth-century French drama, L’Astrée, in which a shepherd named Céladon appears dressed in a gray-green robe. Some modern scholars prefer the term “green ware,” but the poetic French name is more likely to remind a reader that celadon ceramics can exhibit a broad range of colors, from sky blue to sea green.

persona1_adapted:
**What Is Celadon?**

"Celadon" is a name for a large group of high-fired ceramics. High-fired means they are baked in a very hot kiln (a special oven for pottery). 

The glaze on these ceramics gets its color from small amounts of iron oxide (a type of rust). When the kiln has very little oxygen—called a "reducing" atmosphere—the glaze turns into different shades of bluish green. The French word *céladon* describes this color.

Where does the name come from? Most people believe it comes from a 1600s French play called *L’Astrée*. In the play, a shepherd named Céladon wears a gray-green robe.

Some modern scholars prefer the term "green ware." But the poetic French name is better at reminding us that celadon ceramics can show many colors, from sky blue to sea green.

When fired in an oxygen-deprived (or reducing) atmosphere, the glazes turn into various shades of bluish green. This color is traditionally described with the French word *céladon*. The word is commonly believed to come from a seventeenth-century French play, *L’Astrée*. In this play, a shepherd named Céladon appears dressed in a gray-green robe. Some modern scholars prefer the term “green ware.” However, the poetic French name is more likely to remind a reader that celadon ceramics can show a wide range of colors, from sky blue to sea green.

When these ceramics are fired in a kiln with very little oxygen—a process called reduction firing—the glaze turns into various shades of bluish green. This color is traditionally described by the French word *céladon*. The name is thought to come from a 17th-century French play, *L’Astrée*, where a shepherd named Céladon wears a gray-green robe. While some modern scholars prefer the more straightforward term "green ware," the poetic French name helps remind us that celadon ceramics can range widely in color, from sky blue to sea green.

CuratorialText_x: Jade was the first true luxury medium to emerge in ancient China. Although the term is used to refer to a range of hard, beautifully figured stones, most Chinese Neolithic, Bronze Age, and early imperial period examples have been scientifically identified as nephrite, geologically different from the bright, apple green jadeite used in much later times. Composed of interlocked, fibrous crystals and occurring as pebbles and boulders in a range of colors (due to iron, chromium, manganese, or similar materials fused in the rock), nephrite is an incredibly hard stone. It cannot be carved or even scratched by metal but instead must be ground into shape using quartz and other tough minerals. These qualities and its rarity inspired ancient Chinese people to value it very highly and to reserve its use for special purposes, such as for jewelry and ceremonial and ritual objects.

Composed of interlocked, fibrous crystals and occurring as pebbles and boulders in a range of colors (due to iron, chromium, manganese, or similar materials fused in the rock), nephrite is an incredibly hard stone. It cannot be carved or even scratched by metal but instead must be ground into shape using quartz and other tough minerals. These qualities and its rarity inspired ancient Chinese people to value it very highly and to reserve its use for special purposes, such as for jewelry and ceremonial and ritual objects.
persona1_adapted:
Jade was the first truly luxurious material in ancient China. The word "jade" can describe many types of hard, beautiful stones. However, most ancient Chinese jade from the Neolithic, Bronze Age, and early imperial periods is actually nephrite. This is different from the bright, apple-green jadeite used much later.

Nephrite is made of tightly locked, fibrous crystals. It forms as pebbles and boulders in many colors. These colors come from iron, chromium, manganese, or other materials mixed into the rock. Nephrite is extremely hard. You cannot carve it or even scratch it with metal. Instead, people had to grind it into shape using quartz and other tough minerals.

Because nephrite is so rare and hard to work with, ancient Chinese people valued it very highly. They used it only for special purposes, such as jewelry and ceremonial or ritual objects.

CuratorialText_x: According to his close friend Agnes Meyer, when Charles Lang Freer was ill near the end of his life, he often held a piece of jade in his hand, believing with an almost religious faith in the comforting and restorative powers of the stone. Freer collected ancient Chinese jades in large numbers, when most other Western collectors showed little interest. Ultimately, there were more than 500 pieces in his collection, spanning more than 5,000 years.

CuratorialText_x: Green is so prevalent in the flora of the natural world that the color easily conjures lush environments teeming with plants. Life, growth, and regeneration are all concepts that come to mind in the context of verdant landscapes. In Southeast Asia, during the ninth through the thirteenth century, constructing cities required carving out spaces from the verdant, tropical environment. The jungle had to be systematically cultivated; water had to be diverted into reservoirs, moats, and canals; and the landscape had to be carefully tended. When population centers shifted due to political, ecological, and religious changes, the tropical landscape readily encroached on the built environment, transforming once urban landscapes back into jungle. This environment informed Southeast Asian artists: on temple walls and sculptures, plants and flowers grow on thick stalks, vines curlicue, and sprays of foliage transform surfaces into lush landscapes.
CuratorialText_y: Green is so prevalent in the flora of the natural world that the color easily conjures lush environments teeming with plants. Life, growth, and regeneration are all concepts that come to mind in the context of verdant landscapes. The jungle had to be systematically cultivated; water had to be diverted into reservoirs, moats, and canals; and the landscape had to be carefully tended. When population centers shifted due to political, ecological, and religious changes, the tropical landscape readily encroached on the built environment, transforming once urban landscapes back into jungle. This environment informed Southeast Asian artists: on temple walls and sculptures, plants and flowers grow on thick stalks, vines curlicue, and sprays of foliage transform surfaces into lush landscapes.
persona1_adapted:
Green is everywhere in nature. When we see it, we often think of thick forests full of plants. Words like life, growth, and new beginnings come to mind when we picture green landscapes.

In Southeast Asia, from the 800s to the 1200s, building cities meant cutting spaces out of the green, tropical environment. People had to carefully clear the jungle. They had to move water into reservoirs, moats, and canals. They also had to take care of the land.

When cities moved because of changes in politics, nature, or religion, the jungle quickly grew back. It took over the buildings and turned old cities back into forest.

This environment shaped the art of Southeast Asia. On temple walls and sculptures, artists carved plants and flowers with thick stems. Vines twist and curl. Clusters of leaves turn surfaces into rich, green scenes.

Medium: Ink, color wash, and gold on paper
CuratorialText_x: Set in a garden, the image depicts a group of angels, referred to as huris or paris in Persian, entertaining their queen by playing music, dancing, and serving food and wine. The drawing's lively mood echoes other Persian representations of outdoor banquets or bazm (a form of celebration with wine and music) ceremonies, with the exception that this fine drawing features heavenly creatures--a favorite subject matter in sixteenth-and seventeenth-century Iran and Turkey--instead of the princely figures seen in illustrations to lyrical poetry by Hafiz and Assar.

NameOfTheObject: Kneeling Angel with Cup and Bottle

The exquisitely rendered composition typifies a distinct style of Ottoman drawing that combines sweeping, calligraphic lines with soft, feathery strokes, as is evident in the minute designs on the figure's robe, cup, and bottle. Light washes of color add a sense of volume and texture to the form of this heavenly being.

CuratorialText_x: This is one of several paintings of shop fronts that Whistler completed while in St. Ives. Sympathetic critics singled it out for enthusiastic praise when Whistler exhibited it in London in 1884. One declared, it "is probably the most perfect little thing of its kind that was ever wrought by an artist who has learnt how to see….It is without detail, without apparent labour, without dramatic interest; but it is exquisite in colour, faultless in tone, and its well-considered mystery has, at least, the interest of suggestiveness."

NameOfTheObject: Yue ware vessel in the shape of a chimera (bixie)
CuratorialText_x: Molded Yue-ware vessels like this were made in large quantities, but their function is unclear. They may have held a candle, or perhaps scholars dipped their writing brushes in the water-filled interior. In the Jin dynasty (265-420 CE), mythical lions, with exaggeratedly curled manes, and winged chimeras were protective images; huge stone examples guarded important tombs.

During the Jin dynasty (265–420 CE), mythical lions with dramatically curled manes and winged chimeras—creatures combining features of different animals—were seen as protective symbols. Large stone versions of these figures were placed to guard important tombs.

NameOfTheObject: Khusraw at the castle of Shirin, from a manuscript of the Khusraw and Shirin by Nizami (d.1209), recto: illustration: Khusraw at the castle of Shirin; verso: text
Medium: Ink, opaque watercolor and gold on paper
CuratorialText_x:
The Khamsa, or Quintet, by Nizami ranks among the great masterpieces of Persian literature. The romance of Khosraw and Shirin, one of the five poems that constitute the Khamsa, narrates the adventures of the Sasanian king Khusraw Parviz and the Armenian princess Shirin. Attributed to the early fifteenth century, the Freer Khamsa is one of the earliest known illustrated copies of this work.
Khosraw at the Gate of Shirin's Castle is among the most poignantly expressive manuscript paintings of the fifteenth century. Displeased with Khosraw's behavior and his marriage to another woman, Shirin has withdrawn to her castle. Following the death of his queen, Khosraw repeatedly attempts to see Shirin, who finally yields to his request. Shielded by the royal umbrella and standing on precious brocades and carpets, the king gestures in supplication toward Shirin, who gazes down from a window in her castle. Although the text specifies that Khosraw visited Shirin at dawn in the dead of winter, the artist has situated the reconciliation in a lush garden setting softly illuminated by a waning moon. The elegant, mustachioed Khosraw in these paintings bears a striking resemblance to known depictions of the Timurid patron Baysunghur Mirza (1399-1434).

This poem tells the story of a Persian king named Khusraw Parviz and an Armenian princess named Shirin. The Freer *Khamsa* was made in the early 1400s. It is one of the earliest known copies of this work that includes paintings.

**Khosraw at the Gate of Shirin's Castle**

This painting is one of the most emotional manuscript paintings from the 1400s. In the story, Shirin is upset with Khosraw. She is angry because of his behavior and because he married another woman. So she has gone to live in her castle. After his queen dies, Khosraw tries many times to see Shirin. Finally, she agrees to meet him.

In the painting, Khosraw stands under a royal umbrella. He is on expensive carpets and fabrics. He makes a gesture of begging toward Shirin. She looks down at him from a window in her castle.

The story says Khosraw visited Shirin at dawn in the middle of winter. But the artist painted the scene in a lush garden. The garden is softly lit by a waning moon. The elegant Khosraw in this painting has a mustache. He looks very much like known images of the Timurid prince Baysunghur Mirza (1399–1434), who paid for the book.

NameOfTheObject: Harmony in Green and Rose: The Music Room
CuratorialText_x: Set in the London home of Whistler's brother-in-law Seymour Haden, this painting was originally titled The Morning Call. The woman reflected in the mirror at the left is Whistler's half sister Deborah Haden. The seated girl is his niece, Annie Haden. The standing woman, Miss Isabella Boott, was connected to the Haden family by marriage. Instead of trying to create a convincing illusion of three-dimensional space, Whistler adopted a color scheme and sharply angled perspective that emphasize the flatness of the picture plane.

Medium: Opaque watercolor on plain-woven fabric support attached to a fabric covered panel
CuratorialText_x: Popular tales and lively images played a role in transmitting beliefs and values to the Jain community. The Shripalarasa is a narrative that recounts the wondrous adventures of the Jain Prince Shripala, who travels to distant lands in search of trade and wealth. In this unusually large and vivacious painting, the artist represents the Prince's ship as a British vessel. The inscriptions scattered across the painting, which repetively identify parts of the ship, indicate a fascination with seafaring. The depiction of the Union Jack lends immediacy to a story that took place long before the British came to India.

NameOfTheObject: Preparatory drawing for a fan with a scene from the Tale of Shuten Doji
CuratorialText_x: The Tale of Shuten Doji has long been popular in Japan; the earliest-known illustrated version of this tale dates to the fourteenth century. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021), a historical figure of great martial prowess, was transformed in popular literature into a legendary hero who rescued the realm from demonic forces. Shuten Doji, a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto, terrorized the population, dining on locals and holding young maidens captive. With divine assistance and various means of subterfuge, Yorimitsu beheaded the ogre and returned the region to tranquility. Kyosai's interest in the narrative format and his careful connoisseurship of ancient works of art are evident in his paintings and sketchbooks. Riddled with macabre humor and images of the grotesque, his own paintings catered to the tastes of late Edo (1615-1868) and early Meiji (1868-1912) audiences.

CuratorialText_x: Hassam visited Oregon during the summer of 1908, completing this view of Portland's Chinatown after his return to New York. The subject matter is unusual for Hassam, but recalls a series of shop-front scenes painted by Whistler in the 1880s. Like Whistler, Hassam treated the street as a shallow stage set, emphasizing the geometrical patterns created by the play of variously colored rectangular shaped-bricks, signs, windows, doors, walls, sidewalk, alley, and the white diagonal of broken light splayed across the near foreground.

NameOfTheObject: Writing box (suzuribako) executed in relief (makie) and other lacquer techniques
CuratorialText_x: Legendary and theatrical references abound in the decoration of the cover of this lacquer box for an inkstone, a water dropper, and writing brushes. The Wisteria Maiden (Fuji Musume), whose silvery facial features have oxidized to bluish black, is a common subject of Otsu-e, a type of folk painting sold in the town of Otsu near Lake Biwa. In nineteenth-century kabuki, the Wisteria Maiden became the focus of a dance expressing unrequited love to the vocal and instrumental music of a nagauta (literally, "long song"). With her is a praying demon, another figure often seen in Otsu-e. The Thunder God rushes down from above, beating his ring of drums as the couple takes shelter from a rainstorm. The ornate pictorial decoration of this box was created by an artist who had mastered maki-e techniques, which employ powders and particles of gold and other material to create intricate designs.

NameOfTheObject: Bowl with molded and incised design of hibiscus flowers
CuratorialText_x: Bowl, conical, flaring; deep foot with four silica spur-marks underneath on base inside footrim. Six notches in rim. Clay: dense, sonant. Glaze: gray-green celadon; crackled in long lines. Decoration: molded and incised in paste under glaze: rosette in bottom; hibiscus flowers and foliage filling sides: single line incised below rim.
CuratorialText_y: Bowl, conical, flaring; deep foot with four silica spur-marks underneath on base inside footrim. Six notches in rim. Clay: dense, sonant. Glaze: gray-green celadon; crackled in long lines. Decoration: molded and incised in paste under glaze: rosette in bottom; hibiscus flowers and foliage filling sides: single line incised below rim.
persona1_adapted:
This bowl is shaped like a cone that flares outward at the top. It has a deep foot, and on the base inside the footrim, you can see four small spur marks made from silica. The rim has six notches cut into it.

The clay is dense and makes a ringing sound when tapped. The glaze is a gray-green color called celadon. It has long, crackled lines running across it.

The decoration was molded and carved into the clay before the glaze was applied. At the bottom is a rosette design. Hibiscus flowers and leaves fill the sides. A single carved line runs just below the rim.
persona2_adapted: Bowl, conical, flaring; deep foot with four silica spur-marks underneath on the base inside the footrim. Six notches in the rim. Clay: dense, sonant. Glaze: gray-green celadon; crackled in long lines. Decoration: molded and incised in paste under glaze: rosette in the bottom; hibiscus flowers and foliage filling the sides: a single incised line below the rim.
persona3_adapted:
This bowl has a conical shape that flares outward at the top, with a deep foot—the base that supports it. On the underside of the foot, inside the rim, you can see four small marks left by silica spurs, which were used to hold the bowl during firing. The rim itself has six small notches.

The clay body is dense and produces a clear, ringing sound when tapped. The glaze is a gray-green color, known as celadon, and it has developed a pattern of long, fine cracks across the surface. The decoration was created by molding and carving into the clay before the glaze was applied: a rosette (a rose-like design) sits in the center of the bowl’s interior, while hibiscus flowers and leaves fill the sides. A single incised line runs just below the rim.

Medium: Tile; stone-paste body painted with glaze
CuratorialText_x: A rectangular, off-white frit tile with an opaque white underglaze. Overglaze painted with yellow, green, turquoise, blue and manganese black/purple outlines depicting a bird perched on a fruit tree branch. Sides and back are unglazed.
CuratorialText_y: A rectangular, off-white frit tile with an opaque white underglaze. Overglaze painted with yellow, green, turquoise, blue and manganese black/purple outlines depicting a bird perched on a fruit tree branch. Sides and back are unglazed.
persona1_adapted:
This tile is rectangular and off-white. It is made of frit, a type of glass-based ceramic. The tile has an opaque white base layer, called an underglaze.

On top of that layer, the artist painted a bird sitting on a fruit tree branch. The colors used are yellow, green, turquoise, blue, and blackish-purple outlines. The blackish-purple color comes from manganese.

The sides and back of the tile are not glazed.
persona2_adapted: A rectangular frit tile in off-white, with an opaque white underglaze. Overglaze painting uses yellow, green, turquoise, blue, and manganese black/purple outlines. The image shows a bird perched on a branch of a fruit tree. The sides and back are unglazed.
persona3_adapted: This rectangular tile is made from a material called frit—a type of glass-based ceramic that was ground into a powder and then fired. Its surface is a soft off-white, achieved by applying an opaque white layer beneath the main decoration. On top of that layer, the artist painted a scene using vivid colors: yellow, green, turquoise, blue, and a dark purple-black for the outlines. The image shows a bird perched on the branch of a fruit tree. The sides and back of the tile were left unglazed, meaning they were not coated with the glassy finish that gives the front its sheen.

NameOfTheObject: Folio from Yusuf u Zulaykha by Jami (died 1492)

Border: The recto is set in gold, green, and black rulings in a margin with birds, floral, and leaf scrolls. The verso is set in gold, green, and black rulings in a margin with lions, bulls, foxes, rabbits,and birds in a landscape.
CuratorialText_y: Folio from a dispersed copy of Yusuf u Zulaykha by Jami; Persian in black nasta'liq script on gold-flecked paper; heading in red; recto: text: 2 columns, 14 lines; verso: text: 2 columns, 14 lines; one of a group of 13. persona1_adapted:
This page comes from a book called *Yusuf u Zulaykha*, a famous Persian poem by Jami. The text is written in black ink using a script called *nasta'liq*, which is known for its flowing, elegant style. The paper has tiny flecks of gold.

On the front side (called the recto), the text is arranged in two columns with 14 lines. The heading is written in red ink. The back side (the verso) also has two columns and 14 lines. This page is one of 13 similar ones from the same book.

The borders are decorated. On the front, the text is framed by lines of gold, green, and black. Outside those lines, the margin is filled with birds, flowers, and curling leaves. On the back, the margin shows a landscape with lions, bulls, foxes, rabbits, and birds.
persona2_adapted:
Folio from a dispersed copy of *Yusuf u Zulaykha* by Jami.  
The text is written in Persian, in black *nasta'liq* script, on gold-flecked paper.  
The heading is in red.  
Recto: text in two columns, 14 lines.  
Verso: text in two columns, 14 lines.  
This folio is one of a group of 13.  

Border details:  
The recto is framed with rulings in gold, green, and black.  
The margin contains birds, floral motifs, and leaf scrolls.  
The verso is also framed with rulings in gold, green, and black.  
Its margin shows lions, bulls, foxes, rabbits, and birds in a landscape.
persona3_adapted:
This page comes from a scattered copy of *Yusuf u Zulaykha*, a classic Persian poem by the writer Jami. The text is written in black ink in a flowing, elegant script called *nasta'liq*, on paper decorated with flecks of gold. The heading appears in red ink. On both the front and back of the page, the text is arranged in two columns, with fourteen lines per column. This page is one of a set of thirteen.

The front of the page is framed by borders in gold, green, and black, with a margin filled with birds, flowers, and curling leaves. The back of the page has similar gold, green, and black borders, but its margin shows a landscape scene with lions, bulls, foxes, rabbits, and birds.

CuratorialText_x: Squared, hollow cylinder of the type ts’ung [cong] 琮; narrow projecting collar at both ends; rich cream and tans; decoration: incised and in low relief, stylized masks and double bands of fine lines on corners. (Collar chipped.)
CuratorialText_y: Squared, hollow cylinder of the type ts’ung [cong] 琮; narrow projecting collar at both ends; rich cream and tans; decoration: incised and in low relief, stylized masks and double bands of fine lines on corners. (Collar chipped.)
persona1_adapted:
This object is a type of ancient Chinese ritual object called a *ts’ung* (or *cong*). It is shaped like a square tube with a hollow center. Both ends have a narrow, raised rim that sticks out.

The piece is a rich cream color with tan tones. Its decoration is carved into the surface. On the corners, you can see stylized masks—simple, symbolic faces—and double bands made of very fine lines. (Note: The rim is slightly chipped.)
persona2_adapted: Squared, hollow cylinder of the ts’ung [cong] 琮 type. It has a narrow projecting collar at both ends. The colors are rich cream and tans. The decoration is incised and in low relief. It features stylized masks and double bands of fine lines on the corners. (The collar is chipped.)
persona3_adapted: This object is a hollow, square-bodied cylinder with a narrow, projecting rim at both the top and bottom. It belongs to an ancient Chinese type known as a *cong* (pronounced "tsong"). The surface is a rich mix of cream and tan colors. The decoration is carved and lightly raised, featuring stylized mask-like faces and paired bands of fine lines along the corners. (Note: the rim is chipped.)

NameOfTheObject: Vase, one of a pair with F1992.13.2

CuratorialText_x: One of a pair of blue and white vases, Kangxi period, each of trumpet shape form with flared foot, after a bronze Ku, each of the three sections with large loose lotus scroll, the central lobed section encircled by a diaper filled band.

persona1_adapted: These are one of a pair of blue and white vases from the Kangxi period. Each vase has a trumpet-like shape with a wide, flared foot. The design is based on an ancient bronze vessel called a *gu* (a type of drinking cup). The vase is divided into three sections. Each section is decorated with large, flowing lotus flowers. The middle section has a band of repeating diamond patterns, known as a diaper pattern.
persona2_adapted: One of a pair of blue-and-white vases from the Kangxi period. Each vase has a trumpet shape with a flared foot, modeled after a bronze *gu* vessel. persona3_adapted: One of a pair of blue-and-white vases from the Kangxi period (1661–1722). Each vase has a trumpet-shaped body and a flared foot, a form inspired by an ancient Chinese bronze vessel called a *gu*. The vase is divided into three sections, each decorated with large, flowing lotus scrolls. The central, lobed section is encircled by a band filled with a repeating diamond pattern, known as a diaper pattern.

NameOfTheObject: Kenzan-style incense container with design of maple leaves and pampas grass
Medium: Buff clay; white slip, cobalt pigment under transparent glaze
CuratorialText_x: Clay: dense, resonant, buff stoneware. Glaze: transparent, crackled, over white slip; unglazed area stained blackish-brown. Decoration: in cobalt blue, under glaze: linear patterns representing maple leaves and water or pampas grass; geometric bands on sides of lid and base. Square seal on underside of lid.
CuratorialText_y: Clay: dense, resonant, buff stoneware. Glaze: transparent, crackled, over white slip; unglazed area stained blackish-brown. Decoration: in cobalt blue, under glaze: linear patterns representing maple leaves and water or pampas grass; geometric bands on sides of lid and base. Square seal on underside of lid.
persona1_adapted:
**The Object: A Closer Look**

This piece is made from a dense, buff-colored stoneware clay. When you tap it, it makes a deep, ringing sound.

The pot has a shiny, clear glaze with a crackled pattern. This glaze sits on top of a white slip—a thin layer of liquid clay. One area was left unglazed and stained a dark brownish-black.

The decoration is painted in cobalt blue under the glaze. It shows simple, linear patterns that look like maple leaves and water or pampas grass. There are also geometric bands on the sides of the lid and the base.

On the underside of the lid, there is a square seal.
persona2_adapted:
Clay: dense, resonant, buff-colored stoneware.  
Glaze: transparent, with a crackled surface, applied over white slip. The unglazed area is stained blackish-brown.  
Decoration: painted in cobalt blue, under the glaze. The design consists of linear patterns that represent maple leaves and water or pampas grass. Geometric bands appear on the sides of the lid and the base.  
A square seal is located on the underside of the lid.
persona3_adapted:
This object is made of a dense, buff-colored stoneware clay that feels solid and produces a resonant sound when tapped. Its surface is covered with a transparent glaze that has developed a fine network of cracks, a natural effect that occurs over time. Beneath this glaze, the potter applied a white slip—a thin layer of liquid clay—to create a smooth, pale background. One area was left unglazed and has absorbed a dark, blackish-brown stain from use or firing.

The decoration is painted in cobalt blue, a deep blue pigment that was applied before the final glazing. The design consists of flowing, linear patterns that suggest maple leaves and either water or pampas grass. Geometric bands frame the sides of both the lid and the base. On the underside of the lid, a small square seal marks the maker or workshop.

Medium: Porcelain with cobalt pigment under clear glaze

CuratorialText_y: Dish, low, with rounded sides; one chip on rim; hair-line crack. Clay: white porcelain. Glaze: clear, feldspathic; a few pinholes and impurity flecks. Center inside, "three friends of winter" (pine, plum, and bamboo) with garden rock, and fungus, enclosed by double line band which is repeated at rim. Outside, following model, a scene of figures on a garden terrace with plants and trees.

This is a low dish with rounded sides. It has one small chip on the rim and a hairline crack. The dish is made of white porcelain. The glaze is clear and feldspathic—that means it contains a mineral called feldspar. There are a few tiny holes and small specks of dirt in the glaze.

The decoration is painted in a deep blue color called underglaze cobalt blue. This means the blue was painted on the clay before the glaze was applied. The design copies a traditional style from the Ch'eng-hua period, a time in Chinese history.

Inside the dish, at the center, you can see the "three friends of winter": a pine tree, a plum tree, and bamboo. They are shown with a garden rock and a fungus. A double line circles the design, and that same line is repeated around the rim.

On the outside, following the original model, there is a scene of people on a garden terrace with plants and trees.

The decoration is painted in a deep blue pigment applied beneath the glaze, following a traditional design from the Ch'eng-hua period (an era in Chinese history known for its refined ceramics). Medium: Stoneware with clear and rice-straw ash glazes; red lacqer repairs
CuratorialText_x: Individual serving bowl (mukozuke [JPN]). Black lacquer rim. Red lacquer repairs. Clay: hard, light-fawn. Shiny brown on surface. Glaze: yellow and gray over blue-green; inside--brilliant brown and blue. Outside: compounded with iron and rice-straw ash. Inside: clear glaze.
CuratorialText_y: Individual serving bowl (mukozuke [JPN]). Black lacquer rim. Red lacquer repairs. Clay: hard, light-fawn. Shiny brown on surface. Glaze: yellow and gray over blue-green; inside--brilliant brown and blue. Outside: compounded with iron and rice-straw ash. Inside: clear glaze.
persona1_adapted:
This is a **mukozuke**, a type of Japanese bowl used for serving individual portions of food.

The bowl has a black lacquer rim. It was repaired with red lacquer, which is a traditional way to fix broken pottery.

The clay body is hard and light-fawn in color. The surface has a shiny brown finish.

The glaze—a glass-like coating—is yellow and gray on top of a blue-green base. On the inside, the glaze is a brilliant mix of brown and blue.

On the outside, the glaze was made by mixing iron with ash from rice straw. On the inside, a clear glaze was used.
persona2_adapted: Individual serving bowl (mukozuke [JPN]). Rim: black lacquer. Repairs: red lacquer. Clay: hard, light-fawn color. Surface: shiny brown. Glaze: yellow and gray applied over blue-green; interior: brilliant brown and blue. Exterior: compounded with iron and rice-straw ash. Interior: clear glaze.
persona3_adapted:
This small bowl, used for individual servings in Japanese dining, is called a *mukozuke*. Its rim is finished in black lacquer, and red lacquer has been used to repair cracks or chips—a technique that highlights rather than hides the object's history.

The bowl is made from a hard, light tan clay. Its surface has a glossy brown finish. The glaze—a glassy coating applied to the pottery—combines yellow and gray tones over a blue-green base. Inside the bowl, the glaze is a vivid mix of brown and blue.

On the outside, the glaze was created by mixing iron with ash from burned rice straw, a traditional method that gives the surface its distinctive texture and color. Inside, a clear glaze was used, allowing the clay body and the bright colors beneath to show through.

NameOfTheObject: Shiga ware cylindrical tea bowl in Oribe style
Medium: Stoneware with iron glaze and black enamel over clear glaze
CuratorialText_x: White clay, pinkish buff where exposed. Decoration of grasses incised under clear brown wash; hatching incised under clear glaze (body indented in each area); and flower and grasses painted in black overglaze enamel. Kiln mark stamped on base inside foot rim. Thick iron wash over half, appearing dull yellowish brown; clear glaze on other half of body, appearing gray where it partially overlaps wash. Five spur marks (from clay wads) on foot rim.
CuratorialText_y: White clay, pinkish buff where exposed. Decoration of grasses incised under clear brown wash; hatching incised under clear glaze (body indented in each area); and flower and grasses painted in black overglaze enamel. Kiln mark stamped on base inside foot rim. Thick iron wash over half, appearing dull yellowish brown; clear glaze on other half of body, appearing gray where it partially overlaps wash. Five spur marks (from clay wads) on foot rim.
persona1_adapted:
This pot is made of white clay. The parts not covered by a coating have a pinkish-buff color.

The decoration has several layers. First, the artist carved lines into the clay to make a grass pattern. A clear brown wash (a thin, watery coating) was applied over these carved lines. Next, the artist carved more lines, called hatching, under a clear glaze (a glassy coating). The body of the pot is slightly indented in these areas. Finally, flowers and grasses were painted on top using a black enamel (a type of paint that is fired on).

On the base, inside the foot rim (the ring the pot sits on), there is a stamped kiln mark (a maker's mark from the oven where it was fired).

Half of the pot has a thick iron wash that looks dull yellowish-brown. The other half has a clear glaze that looks gray where it partly overlaps the iron wash. There are five spur marks (small bumps from clay supports) on the foot rim.
persona2_adapted: White clay, pinkish buff where exposed. Decoration includes: grasses incised under a clear brown wash; hatching incised under clear glaze (the body is indented in each area); and flowers and grasses painted in black overglaze enamel. A kiln mark is stamped on the base inside the foot rim. A thick iron wash covers half of the piece, appearing as a dull yellowish brown. The other half of the body has a clear glaze, which appears gray where it partially overlaps the wash. There are five spur marks (from clay wads) on the foot rim.
persona3_adapted: This piece is made from white clay, which takes on a pinkish-buff color where the surface is left exposed. The decoration includes grasses scratched into the clay beneath a clear brown wash, as well as hatched lines—closely spaced parallel marks—carved under a clear glaze, with the body of the piece slightly indented in each area. Flowers and grasses are painted on top of the glaze in black enamel. A kiln mark is stamped on the base, inside the foot rim. Half of the piece is covered with a thick iron wash, which appears dull yellowish-brown; the other half has a clear glaze that looks gray where it partially overlaps the wash. On the foot rim, there are five spur marks—small scars left by clay supports used during firing.

NameOfTheObject: Tree studies: illustrations from Nuzhatnama-yi ala'i (Book of Council) by Shahmardan ibn Abi al-Khayr; recto: Medicinal property of the mulberry tree; verso: Pharmacological property of the olive tree, its leaves and fruits
CuratorialText_x: Mounted with F1937.39. Detached folio from a dispersed copy of Nuzhatnama-yi ala'i (Excellent book of counsel) by Shahmardan ibn Abi al-Khayr; text: Persian in black and red naskhi script; recto: illustration and text: Medicinal property of the mulberry tree, 2 lines, 3 paintings; verso: illustration and text: Pharmacological property of the olive tree, its leaves and fruits, 4 lines, 2 paintings; one of a group of three folios.
CuratorialText_y: Mounted with F1937.39. Detached folio from a dispersed copy of Nuzhatnama-yi ala'i (Excellent book of counsel) by Shahmardan ibn Abi al-Khayr; text: Persian in black and red naskhi script; recto: illustration and text: Medicinal property of the mulberry tree, 2 lines, 3 paintings; verso: illustration and text: Pharmacological property of the olive tree, its leaves and fruits, 4 lines, 2 paintings; one of a group of three folios.
persona1_adapted:
This page is part of a book called *Nuzhatnama-yi 'Ala'i* (The Excellent Book of Counsel), written by Shahmardan ibn Abi al-Khayr. The text is in Persian, written in black and red ink using a script called *naskhi*.

On the front side (recto) is an illustration and text about the medicinal uses of the mulberry tree. It has 2 lines of text and 3 paintings.

On the back side (verso) is an illustration and text about the pharmacological (medical) properties of the olive tree, including its leaves and fruits. This side has 4 lines of text and 2 paintings.

This page is one of three similar pages that were once part of a larger book, but are now separated.
persona2_adapted: Mounted with F1937.39. This is a detached folio from a dispersed copy of the *Nuzhatnama-yi ala'i* (Excellent Book of Counsel) by Shahmardan ibn Abi al-Khayr. On the recto: there is an illustration and text describing the medicinal property of the mulberry tree, consisting of 2 lines and 3 paintings. On the verso: there is an illustration and text describing the pharmacological property of the olive tree, including its leaves and fruits, consisting of 4 lines and 2 paintings. persona3_adapted: This folio, now separated from its original book, comes from a manuscript called the *Nuzhatnama-yi ‘Ala’i* (or *Excellent Book of Counsel*), written by Shahmardan ibn Abi al-Khayr. The text is in Persian, written in a clear, upright script known as *naskhi*, with black and red ink. On the back side, the illustration and text explain the pharmacological properties of the olive tree—its leaves and fruits—with four lines of writing and two paintings. NameOfTheObject: Folio from Aja'ib al-makhluqat (Wonders of Creation) by al-Qazvini; recto: Constellations of the Southern Hemisphere: Corvus (al-Ghurab), Centaurus (Qanturis); verso: Lupus (al-Saba'), Ara (al-Mijmara), Corona Austrina
Medium: Opaque watercolor, ink and gold on paper
Dimension: H x W (recto bird): 4.1 × 6.8 cm (1 5/8 × 2 11/16 in)
CuratorialText_x: Both Corvus, "The Raven," and Centaurus, "The Centaur," are constellations of the southern hemisphere. Corvus is composed of seven stars while Centaurus, whose forepart is made up of a man and hindpart of a horse, consists of 37. Al-Kazwini records that the last two stars of Centaurus are called "the two sworn upon," and according to tradition one of these passes very close to the path of Corvus. Some people, writes al-Kazwini, believe that the star belongs to "the Raven" while others insist that it is part of "The Centaur."

Some people, writes al-Kazwini, believe that the star belongs to "the Raven" while others insist that it is part of "The Centaur."

Both Corvus (the Raven) and Centaurus (the Centaur) are constellations in the southern sky. Corvus has seven stars. Centaurus has 37 stars. Its front half looks like a man, and its back half looks like a horse.

A scholar named Al-Kazwini wrote that the last two stars of Centaurus are called "the two sworn upon." According to tradition, one of these stars passes very close to the path of Corvus. Al-Kazwini says some people believe this star belongs to the Raven. Others insist it is part of the Centaur.

Al-Kazwini writes that some people believe this star belongs to "The Raven," while others insist it is part of "The Centaur."

NameOfTheObject: Vase shaped like an archaic gu
CuratorialText_x: Vessel in the shape of a Shang dynasty ceremonial "ku;" copper decorated with cloisonne enamels in light blue, dark blue, green, red, yellow, and white; inside the foot is an inscription of eight characters in relief giving a K'ang-hsi date. A separate flower holder fits inside the mouth. During the Qing dynasty (1644-1911), the display of ancient objects as well as contemporary ones made in an archaistic style was considered a sign of learning and refined taste. The shape and decoration of this vase, including the mask-like faces on the base, reproduce aspects of a Bronze Age ritual vessel called a gu.
CuratorialText_y: Vessel in the shape of a Shang dynasty ceremonial "ku;" copper decorated with cloisonne enamels in light blue, dark blue, green, red, yellow, and white; inside the foot is an inscription of eight characters in relief giving a K'ang-hsi date. A separate flower holder fits inside the mouth. During the Qing dynasty (1644-1911), the display of ancient objects as well as contemporary ones made in an archaistic style was considered a sign of learning and refined taste. The shape and decoration of this vase, including the mask-like faces on the base, reproduce aspects of a Bronze Age ritual vessel called a gu.
persona1_adapted:
This vase is shaped like an ancient Chinese wine cup called a *gu* (pronounced "goo"). It is made of copper and decorated with colorful enamel. The colors include light blue, dark blue, green, red, yellow, and white. Inside the base, there is an inscription with eight raised Chinese characters. These characters tell us the vase was made during the Kangxi period (1661–1722).

A separate flower holder fits inside the vase's opening. During the Qing dynasty (1644–1911), displaying old objects—or new ones made to look old—was a sign of learning and good taste. The shape and decoration of this vase copy parts of a Bronze Age ritual vessel called a *gu*. For example, the mask-like faces on the base are copied from those ancient bronze vessels.

It is made of copper and decorated with cloisonné enamel in light blue, dark blue, green, red, yellow, and white. Inside the foot, there is an inscription of eight characters in relief. This inscription gives a date from the Kangxi period.

A separate flower holder fits inside the mouth of the vessel.

During the Qing dynasty (1644–1911), displaying ancient objects—as well as contemporary objects made in an archaistic style—was considered a sign of learning and refined taste.

The shape and decoration of this vase, including the mask-like faces on the base, reproduce aspects of a Bronze Age ritual vessel called a *gu*.
persona3_adapted:
This vessel takes the form of a *gu*, a type of ritual wine container from China’s Bronze Age. It is made of copper and decorated with *cloisonné* enamel—a technique in which thin metal wires are used to create small compartments, or cells, that are then filled with colored enamel paste and fired. Here, the colors include light and dark blue, green, red, yellow, and white. Inside the foot of the vessel is an inscription of eight raised characters, which records a date from the reign of the Kangxi Emperor (1661–1722). A separate flower holder fits inside the mouth of the vase.

During the Qing dynasty (1644–1911), displaying ancient objects—as well as contemporary works made in an antique style—was seen as a mark of learning and sophisticated taste. The shape and decoration of this vase, including the mask-like faces on its base, deliberately echo features of a Bronze Age ritual vessel known as a *gu*.

NameOfTheObject: Waves at Matsushima 松島図屏風
Medium: Ink, color, gold, and silver on paper
Dimension: H x W (overall [each]): 166 x 369.9 cm (65 3/8 x 145 5/8 in)
CuratorialText_x:
The brilliant paintings on this pair of folding screens are regarded as masterpieces among only six surviving sets of screens by Sotatsu, a talented and innovative artist who headed a painting workshop known as Tawaraya. As a townsman, Sotatsu produced paintings such as fans for popular consumption. By the late 1620s, however, Sotatsu was painting for the imperial court, and his works survive in the collections of the Kyoto imperial palace. For his artistic merit, he was granted the honorary Buddhist ecclesiastical title Hokkyo (Bridge of the Law), which is included in his signature on these screens.
Matsushima (Pine Islands) is the name of a famous site (meisho) near Sendai, a city in Miyagi Prefecture in northeastern Japan. Its beauty inspired both poets and painters. In Sotatsu's screens the rock from which pine trees grow are rendered in brilliant mineral colors of green, blue, and brown, highlighted with gold. Waves in animated forms are delineated in alternating lines of ink and gold, producing a luminous effect. Clouds and embankments are rendered in particles of gold leaf accented with silver, which has darkened over time to a soft black tone.
Sotatsu's innovative composition creates a dynamic interplay among the land and cloud forms, the bending pines, and churning waves. His lifelong interest in pictorial composition left a lasting legacy. Later painters of the Rimpa school, such as Ogata Korin (1658-1716), repeated the Matsushima theme in their work. The Matsushima screens were probably painted in the early Kan'ei era (1624-44), a period of cultural efflorescence in Kyoto.

*Matsushima* (Pine Islands) is the name of a famous scenic site near Sendai, a city in Miyagi Prefecture in northeastern Japan. Its beauty inspired both poets and painters. In Sotatsu's screens, the rocks from which pine trees grow are rendered in vivid mineral colors—green, blue, and brown—highlighted with gold. Waves in lively, swirling forms are outlined with alternating lines of ink and gold, creating a glowing effect. Clouds and embankments are made with particles of gold leaf, accented with silver that has darkened over time to a soft black tone.

Sotatsu's inventive composition creates a dynamic interplay among the land and cloud forms, the bending pines, and the churning waves. His lifelong interest in pictorial composition left a lasting influence. Later painters of the Rimpa school, such as Ogata Korin (1658–1716), repeated the Matsushima theme in their own work. The Matsushima screens were likely painted in the early Kan'ei era (1624–44), a period of cultural flourishing in Kyoto.

NameOfTheObject: Preparatory drawing for a fan with a scene from the Tale of Shuten Doji
CuratorialText_x:
The Tale of Shuten Doji has long been popular in Japan; the earliest-known illustrated version of this tale dates to the fourteenth century. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021), a historical figure of great martial prowess, was transformed in popular literature into a legendary hero who rescued the realm from demonic forces. Shuten Doji, a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto, terrorized the population, dining on locals and holding young maidens captive. With divine assistance and various means of subterfuge, Yorimitsu beheaded the ogre and returned the region to tranquility.
Kyosai's interest in the narrative format and his careful connoisseurship of ancient works of art are evident in his paintings and sketchbooks. Riddled with macabre humor and images of the grotesque, his own paintings catered to the tastes of late Edo (1615-1868) and early Meiji (1868-1912) audiences.

NameOfTheObject: Dagger-axe (ge 戈) with masks (taotie) and dragons
Medium: Bronze with turquoise inlay and jade (nephrite) blade

CuratorialText_x: Ceremonial sickle in four parts: three of bronze inlaid with turquoise, one (the blade) of jade decorated in linear relief with notched back and ground edge. No objects of this type have yet been scientifically excavated in China, so it is difficult to know the purpose of this extremely rare axe. It consists of four separate pieces: a tubular bronze handle, two caps with dragon motifs, and an unusual serrated jade blade that might not be original. Traces of wood inside the handle suggest it was lengthened with additional elements that are now missing.

NameOfTheObject: The Tale of Genji, Chapter 51; Ukifune (one of a pair with F1965.6)
CuratorialText_x: This is one of a pair of folding screens illustrating two of the fifty-four chapters in The Tale of Genji, a narrative composed in the early eleventh century by a lady of the court known as Murasaki Shikibu. This fictional biography of Prince Genji, whose life was filled with romance and tragedy, was a frequent subject of Japanese paintings. This screen represents chapter fifty-one, Ukifune. The lady Ukifune and her lover Niou, drift together in a boat on a wintry day, sheltered by a folding screen and temporarily isolated in their tragic romance. The painter Kano Naonobu chose monochromatic ink and thin color washes for a theme that was more commonly illustrated in lavish color and gold. The dreamlike effect of the mists and washes is poetically evocative of the mood of each episode. Naonobu was an official painter who served the Tokugawa shogun.

persona1_adapted:
This jar is a type of ancient Chinese pottery called a "hill-jar." It has a round, cylinder-shaped body and a lid. Three small feet shaped like bears support the bottom. You can see small marks left from the kiln, where it was fired.

The jar is made from reddish clay with a sandy feel. It was fired at a medium temperature. A lead-based glaze gives it a deep green color. In some spots, the glaze has a shiny, silvery look. There are also bits of dirt and minerals crusted on the surface from being buried.

The decoration is pressed into the clay in raised patterns. Horizontal bands show animals and people on horseback. There are also two fake handles shaped like rings and masks. The lid is shaped like a mountain, with more animal figures raised on its surface.

Chinese Han Dynasty Glazed Pottery "Hill-Jar"

The glaze is made of lead silicate. It is deep green, with areas of silvery iridescence. There are also earth incrustations on the surface.

The decoration consists of horizontal panels. These panels are molded in relief and show animals and horsemen. There are two simulated mask-and-ring handles. The cover represents mountains, with animals also shown in relief.

The reddish, sandy clay was fired at a medium temperature. The deep green glaze is made from lead and silica, and over time it has developed patches of silvery shimmer and earthy crusts from being buried. The decoration includes horizontal bands pressed into the clay, showing animals and horsemen in raised relief. Two handles are shaped to look like masks holding rings. The lid represents a mountain landscape, also decorated with raised animal figures.

CuratorialText_x: Dive into the artistic process of one of history’s most beloved and influential artists. A true titan of the Italian Renaissance, Raffaello di Giovanni Santi (1483–1520)—better known as Raphael—matched ambition with lyricism to create works with both intellectual heft and emotional depth, a necessary skill in the complex political landscape of Renaissance courts. In his short life of only 37 years, he achieved such profound success as a painter, designer, and architect that he was regarded as the pinnacle of artistic perfection for centuries after his death. Raphael: Sublime Poetry is the first comprehensive exhibition on Raphael in the United States, bringing together more than 170 of the artist’s greatest masterpieces and rarely seen treasures to illuminate the brilliance of Raphael’s extraordinary creativity. The son of a painter and poet, Raphael engaged with the foremost writers and thinkers of his age in Rome, displaying a poetic sensibility that captivated his peers and generations that followed. Follow the full breadth of his life and career, from his origins in Urbino to his rise in Florence, where he began to emerge as a peer of Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo, to his final, prolific decade at the papal court in Rome. To underscore the range of his genius, this presentation brings together important drawings, paintings, and tapestries from public and private collections across Europe and the United States, many of which have never been shown together. With particular attention to Raphael’s portrayal of women—from his use of nude female models for the first time in Western art to his tender depictions of the Madonna and Child—and recent scientific discoveries made using state-of-the-art technology, this exhibition offers a rare opportunity to experience the genius of an artist who helped shape the course of art history.

persona1_adapted:
**Meet Raphael: A Star of the Renaissance**

Step into the world of one of history’s most famous and influential artists. Raffaello di Giovanni Santi (1483–1520)—better known as Raphael—was a giant of the Italian Renaissance. He combined big ideas with beautiful, emotional images. This skill was important in the tricky political world of Renaissance courts.

In his short life of just 37 years, Raphael became a huge success as a painter, designer, and architect. For centuries after his death, people saw him as the perfect artist.

**The Exhibition: *Raphael: Sublime Poetry***

This is the first major exhibition of Raphael’s work in the United States. It brings together over 170 of his greatest pieces and rare treasures. Together, they show the brilliance of Raphael’s amazing creativity.

Raphael was the son of a painter and a poet. In Rome, he worked with the top writers and thinkers of his time. His work had a poetic feel that amazed his peers and many generations after them.

**His Life and Career**

Follow the full story of his life and work. It starts in his hometown of Urbino. Then it moves to Florence, where he began to match the skills of Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo. Finally, it ends in Rome, where he spent his last, most productive years working for the pope.

To show the range of his genius, this exhibition brings together important drawings, paintings, and tapestries. These come from public and private collections across Europe and the United States. Many of them have never been shown together before.

**What Makes This Exhibition Special**

The exhibition pays special attention to how Raphael painted women. For example, he was one of the first Western artists to use nude female models. He also created tender images of the Madonna and Child (Mary and baby Jesus).

In addition, the show includes recent scientific discoveries made with modern technology. This exhibition offers a rare chance to experience the genius of an artist who helped shape the course of art history.

CuratorialText_x: One of the greatest influencers of all time, Raphael (1483 –1520) was idolized for more than three centuries, in Europe and beyond, as the painter of supreme perfection and the role model for artists in training. During his short life, his contemporaries celebrated his enormous creative facility, and later his fame towered above that of other Italian Renaissance stars. Born Raffaello di Giovanni Santi, the ambitious young artist soon left behind his modest origins in the Marche region of central Italy in search of important patrons in Umbria and Tuscany. In Florence, then the capital of the European art world, he emerged as a peer to the elder Leonardo and Michelangelo. Raphael spent his final decade as the favorite artist of the popes in Rome, where he was praised as the “prince of painters.” A savvy businessman and impresario, he worked with well-organized teams of assistants and collaborators to propagate his inventions and style, spawning a new generation of noteworthy artists.

During his short life, other artists praised his huge creative talent. Later, his fame grew even greater than other famous Italian Renaissance stars. Born Raffaello di Giovanni Santi, he was an ambitious young artist. He left his modest home in the Marche region of central Italy. He went looking for important supporters in Umbria and Tuscany.

CuratorialText_x: This first comprehensive U.S. exhibition on Raphael provides an immersive look at his meteoric career. CuratorialText_x: Raphael received his earliest training from his father, Giovanni Santi (ca. 1439–1494), a court painter and prolific poet. During Raphael’s childhood, Giovanni was busy composing an epic poem on Federico III da Montefeltro (1422–1482), the famous ruler who had brought peace, wealth, and fame to the Duchy of Urbino. A formidable general, scholar, and patron of art and architecture, Federico transformed his court into an internationally celebrated center for the arts and humanist learning. He founded a fabled library and rebuilt Urbino’s imposing Palazzo Ducale, whose rhythmic arcades and harmonic geometry embodied the humanist dedication to mathematics that distinguished the culture of Urbino. Those same ideals are evident in the vibrant architectural visions—enlivened with perspectival effects and classical references—created by Urbino painters.

CuratorialText_x: Raphael’s father soon brought his precocious son to study with Pietro Perugino (1446/50–1523), an artist he likely knew through local projects. A brilliant, prolific painter and draftsman with active workshops in Perugia and Florence, Perugino relied heavily on collaboration with assistants to meet the demands of patrons all over Italy. Raphael coursed quickly through the stages of pupil and apprentice to become first a collaborator to Perugino and then an independent artist. Perugino’s elegant figures, breathtaking command of technique, and efficient methods for reproducing designs left their mark on the younger artist.

CuratorialText_x: Ambitious, studious, and disciplined, the young Raphael first sought to establish himself as an independent professional in Città di Castello, a small, wealthy town near Perugia, where he painted notable church altarpieces. Confraternities, religious institutions for regular citizens devoted to works of charity, were also major sources of patronage for artists in Umbria and the Marche. Gathered here are paintings and related studies done for confraternities by Perugino and the young Raphael—including the first painting fully in Raphael’s hand, as confirmed by its recent conservation treatment.

CuratorialText_x: From 1500 to 1507, the young Raphael endeavored to cultivate patrons by painting both monumental altarpieces and small-scale devotional works in the regions of the Marche, Umbria, and Tuscany. An iconic example is the large, multipart painting at the center of this gallery, made for nuns in Perugia and now known as the Colonna Altarpiece (after a later owner). The full ensemble is reunited here as a complete work for the first time since it was dismembered around 1663 and the individual paintings dispersed. Other notable projects include a mammoth altarpiece for the Baronci family chapel in Città di Castello, which was later destroyed in an earthquake and survives only in fragments and preliminary drawings, and a remarkable altarpiece for the Oddi family chapel in Perugia, represented here by studies and the painting that provided its base.

CuratorialText_x: Raphael may have been drawn to Florence after hearing fellow painters rave about rival full-scale designs (cartoons) for spectacular murals there by Leonardo and Michelangelo. Related works in this gallery convey the electrifying novelty and arresting sculptural power of those unrealized projects. While in Florence between 1504 and 1508, Raphael also studied Michelangelo’s sculptures and Leonardo’s paintings and drawings, promptly and seamlessly adapting aspects of their styles and techniques in his work.

CuratorialText_x: Raphael also learned from his peers’ revolutionary approaches to inventing compositions. Leonardo’s theories about “sketching quickly,” in the fervor of artistic inspiration, transformed Raphael’s methods. Asserting that the effort to finish drawings and puzzle out details could dry up fresh ideas, Leonardo advised working out concepts in “rough sketches,” like a poet scrawling the first draft of a verse in messy handwriting with crossed-out words. Before Florence, Raphael had tended to develop compositions in a more artisanal way, figure by detailed figure, as he had learned from Perugino. Now, spontaneous sketching led him to more organic compositions pulsating with movement and vivid expression.

Now, spontaneous sketching led him to more organic compositions pulsating with movement and vivid expression.

Before moving to Florence, Raphael made art in a more careful, step-by-step way. He drew one figure in detail at a time, just as his teacher Perugino had taught him. But after learning from Leonardo, Raphael started sketching freely. This helped him create more natural compositions. His art became full of movement and strong emotion.

CuratorialText_x: The exposure to Leonardo’s and Michelangelo’s art infused Raphael’s compositions with a sense of space, sculptural monumentality, and expressive force. This was the result of an enormous amount of experimentation on paper. Sequences of drawings—from a sheet of sketches to studies of an arrangement based on three-dimensional clay or wax models—reveal Raphael’s increasingly disciplined approach to the design process. Working in orderly steps, for example, he gradually animated a pyramidal composition (favored by Leonardo), which could seem static, by building a dynamic interplay of gestures and gazes among the figures.

Raphael made many drawings in a series. He started with quick sketches. Then he studied how to arrange figures using small clay or wax models. These steps show how Raphael became more careful and organized in his design process.

For example, he worked step by step to bring life to a pyramid-shaped composition. (Leonardo liked this shape.) A pyramid can look still or boring. But Raphael made it feel active by building a lively give-and-take of gestures and looks between the figures.

Working in orderly steps, for example, he gradually brought life to a pyramidal composition (a structure favored by Leonardo), which could otherwise appear static. He did this by building a dynamic interplay of gestures and gazes among the figures.

This came from a huge amount of experimentation on paper. By looking at series of drawings—from quick sketches to studies based on small clay or wax models—we can see how Raphael developed a more organized and controlled way of working. For example, he took a pyramid-shaped arrangement of figures (a structure Leonardo often used) and, step by step, brought it to life. He added a lively exchange of gestures and glances among the figures, turning what could have been a still, balanced scene into something more active and engaging.

The preliminary drawings show him in full command of his powers as a storyteller, and demonstrate how he constructed a dramatic composition with a climactic narrative. Through arduous study, aided by his knowledge of classical sculpture, the artist evolved a static arrangement of figures into an emotionally arresting action scene. Raphael had absorbed Leonardo’s principle that the physical gestures of the body express the “motions” of the mind and soul.

CuratorialText_x: For centuries Raphael has been widely admired for Madonna and Child paintings that include a display of tenderness. From about 1300, Byzantine icons of the Eastern Orthodox faith had featured a form of the Madonna known as the Virgin Eleousa, from the Greek for “compassionate,” in which Mary embraces the Christ Child as he touches his cheek to hers. Disseminated widely, the imagery inspired numerous medieval and Renaissance artists, including Italian sculptors and painters. Raphael drew upon this model and thoroughly transformed it.

CuratorialText_x: Raphael’s ability to capture a mood of vitality and innocent playfulness, especially while maintaining a superior command of anatomical realism, is striking in light of the historical realities of childbirth, infancy, and parenthood in his time. The rare account book displayed here records funerary expenses for Raphael’s mother, who died of childbirth-related complications in 1491, and for two of his siblings who died in infancy. Knowledge of gynecology and obstetrics was scarce, pregnancy and childbirth extremely dangerous, and infant mortality rates astronomically high. These facts, along with a desire among Renaissance artists to humanize religious subjects, all contributed to the appeal of the tender Madonna and Child as votive figures and subjects in art.

These facts, along with a desire among Renaissance artists to humanize religious subjects, all contributed to the appeal of the tender Madonna and Child as votive figures and subjects in art.
persona1_adapted:

The rare account book on display here lists funeral costs for Raphael’s mother. She died from childbirth problems in 1491. It also lists costs for two of his siblings who died as babies.

Back then, people knew very little about women’s health and childbirth. Pregnancy and giving birth were very dangerous. Many babies did not survive.

These facts help explain why paintings of the Madonna and Child were so popular. Renaissance artists wanted to make religious subjects feel more human. So tender images of mother and child became common in art.

These difficult facts, combined with a desire among Renaissance artists to make religious subjects feel more human and relatable, all help explain why the tender image of the Madonna and Child became so popular—both as objects of devotion and as subjects for art.

CuratorialText_x: Despite his great ambitions, Raphael was largely thwarted in his attempts to break into the competitive art market in Florence. He found his most feasible source of patronage among the city’s wealthy merchants, who commissioned portraits and tender devotional paintings, mainly of the Madonna and Child. Raphael continued to produce works on this subject for private patrons well after his move to Rome in 1508, and many of his collaborators followed his example.

CuratorialText_x: Raphael sought to bring the lauded ethereal physical beauty of the Madonna into radiant being. He was not immune to the convention, sung by poets both within and beyond his circle, that favored an elegant, aristocratic lady with dainty facial features and golden hair, but he also strove for long-established Christian ideals. Sermons, prayers, songs, and poetry inspired by early church writings had praised the Virgin’s beauty and grace as mirrors of her unique spiritual perfection. He infused his representations with a humanity and psychological presence revealed through gestures and reactions. Raphael’s paintings display an arresting command of light, color, space, and geometry, and his renderings of infants are more naturalistic in their proportions, poses, and expressions than those of many of his peers.

His paintings demonstrate a striking mastery of light, color, space, and geometric composition. And his depictions of infants are more lifelike in their proportions, poses, and expressions than those of many other artists of his time.

CuratorialText_x: Raphael’s portraits communicate a profound empathy and reflect years of exercising his hand in drawings to achieve an intimate, concerted contemplation of the sitter. The regal pose of Leonardo’s Mona Lisa, begun in 1503, offered a revolutionary model for the likeness of a luxuriously dressed person: a three-quarter view, head turned to the left, torso erect, and hands resting gracefully at the waist. The elegance of the poses here also suggests a striving for the cultivated manners of the Italian Renaissance courts and the ideals of beauty celebrated by famous poets. Raphael was an intimate friend of Baldassarre Castiglione (1478–1529), whose book on fashionable behavior at court promoted a model of comportment and grace that prized sprezzatura, a studied nonchalance or insouciance.

The elegant poses in Raphael’s work also suggest a desire for the polished manners of Italian Renaissance courts. They reflect the ideals of beauty praised by famous poets.

Raphael was a close friend of Baldassarre Castiglione (1478–1529). Castiglione wrote a book about fashionable behavior at court. It promoted a model of grace and good manners that valued *sprezzatura*—a kind of studied effortlessness or casual coolness.

CuratorialText_x: Many Renaissance portraits of women were painted at the time of betrothal and marriage, a financial-dynastic arrangement in which romantic love had no place. Elegant fashion and jewelry were meant to impress the future husband’s family, as dowries and property were negotiated. Overt appraisals of women’s appearances by poets and artists also contributed to a culture in which young women could become innamorate—platonic beloveds or actual mistresses—of a male aristocracy.
CuratorialText_y: Many Renaissance portraits of women were painted at the time of betrothal and marriage, a financial-dynastic arrangement in which romantic love had no place. Elegant fashion and jewelry were meant to impress the future husband’s family, as dowries and property were negotiated. Overt appraisals of women’s appearances by poets and artists also contributed to a culture in which young women could become innamorate—platonic beloveds or actual mistresses—of a male aristocracy.
persona1_adapted: During the Renaissance, many portraits of women were painted when they got engaged or married. Back then, marriage was often about money and family power, not romantic love. The women wore fancy clothes and jewelry to impress their future husband’s family while families discussed dowries and property. Poets and artists also often praised women’s looks. This helped create a culture where young women could become *innamorate*—meaning a man’s platonic love interest or actual mistress—among the wealthy and powerful men of the time.

This contributed to a culture in which young women could become *innamorate*—either platonic beloveds or actual mistresses—of a male aristocracy.
