CuratorialText_x: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also developed into a renowned painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced the Mannerism of Bartholomäus Spranger to the Netherlands. The style is recognizable by muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads in intricate compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of the Italian masters. Back in the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind and opted for a more classicist style with classical proportions in his work. His technically complex engravings, in particular, were admired throughout Europe. Goltzius's oeuvre provides a fine reflection of the developments in Dutch painting around 1600.

CuratorialText_x: Visual artist and photographer Viviane Sassen (1972) visited the village of Pikin Slee, deep in the rainforest of Suriname, in 2012. Here, people live without running water, electricity, roads, or the internet. But however remote the village may be, the influence of the modern world is clearly noticeable. For instance, this community also possesses mobile phones and plastic household items. The photo series that Sassen created in Pikin Slee captures the beauty of everyday life in abstract compositions. Largely in black and white, but with the colorful splashes characteristic of Sassen's earlier work. In 2013, the Pikin Slee series was added to the collection of the Rijksmuseum as a modern addition to the collection of photographs of Suriname, which until then had been primarily historical in character.

CuratorialText_x: William of Orange (1533–1584) was an ambitious nobleman who rose to become the leader of the Revolt and was revered as the 'father of the fatherland'. At the age of 11, William inherited the French principality of Orange and received the title 'Prince of Orange'. He led a turbulent life with an impressive career. Philip II, the son of Charles V, appointed William as Stadtholder of Holland, Zeeland, and Utrecht, the most important Dutch provinces. The two men clashed. Philip strived for an empire with central authority and one religion: Roman Catholicism. William, on the other hand, stood for freedom of religion and conscience for everyone. This struggle cost him his life on July 10, 1584, from a gunshot.

CuratorialText_x: Who is that girl in the painting by Simon Maris, anyway? Little is known about her. However, it is clear that around the year 1906, she modeled for Simon Maris in his studio at Keizersgracht 498. She was about 12 years old at the time. This is evident from photographs Maris took of the young lady in his studio. Documents have been preserved in Simon Maris's archive in which he refers to her as 'Isabella'. Some other paintings Maris made of the girl were also given this name. Nevertheless, it is not certain that this young lady was really named 'Isabella'. It is possible that Maris invented this name for her. Or perhaps she used a pseudonym herself, as models often did at that time.

CuratorialText_x: Amalia of Solms (1602-1675) played a crucial role in increasing the prestige of the House of Orange and the international positioning of the Netherlands. She serves as a sounding board and advisor to her husband, Stadtholder Frederik Hendrik. When he falls ill, she assumes some of his duties and charts a course that heralds the end of the Eighty Years' War. Three years after her husband's death, her son passes away, marking the beginning of the first stadtholderless era. To safeguard the future of the House of Orange, Van Solms exerts national and international influence. She uses art as a political tool. Her collection of paintings, furniture, and Asian objects competes with other European courts. In doing so, she firmly establishes the Netherlands, with the House of Orange at its head, as a European center of art and trade.

CuratorialText_x: Between 1650 and 1672, there was no Stadtholder. The most powerful citizens governed the country. As Grand Pensionary of Holland, Johan de Witt rose to become the most influential politician. He cunningly plays England and France, the Republic's enemies, against each other. Nevertheless, these countries, together with the prince-bishoprics of Münster and Cologne, attack the Netherlands in 1672. His brother, Cornelis de Witt, oversees the military activities as a deputy of the States General. As the foreign troops gain ground, the call for the return of the Stadtholder grows ever louder. Cornelis is arrested on suspicion of conspiracy against the Prince of Orange. When Johan visits him in prison, an incited mob drags the brothers out and kills them. Long after their deaths, both are remembered as champions of 'true freedom'.

CuratorialText_x: At the beginning of the 19th century, Hortense de Beauharnais (1783-1837) and Louis Napoleon became the first Queen and King of Holland, appointed by Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte. The royal couple had three sons together, including the future Emperor Napoleon III, but it was an unhappy marriage. Queen Hortense was often present at festivities and official occasions and, partly due to her visits to local businesses, became increasingly popular with the people. That was until her husband, displeased with her popularity, forced her into the background. After Napoleon's defeat, she settled in Switzerland without her husband. At her impressive Arenenberg estate, De Beauharnais, herself a talented musician and composer, organized cultural gatherings that were talked about for a long time afterwards.

The royal couple had three sons together. One of them became the future Emperor Napoleon III. But their marriage was not a happy one.

Queen Hortense often appeared at parties and official events. She also visited local businesses. Because of this, she became more and more popular with the people. But her husband did not like her popularity. He forced her to stay out of the spotlight.

After Napoleon was defeated, Hortense moved to Switzerland without her husband. She lived at her impressive estate called Arenenberg. Hortense was a talented musician and composer. She organized cultural gatherings there that people talked about for a long time afterward.

CuratorialText_x: On July 13, 1885, the new Rijksmuseum officially opened its doors after a construction period of nearly ten years. The building was designed by the architect Pierre Cuypers (1827–1921), a prominent member of the Catholic Church. On the exterior of the Rijksmuseum, Cuypers depicted himself, just as the master builders did in the decoration of cathedrals during the Middle Ages. Over the years, many original decorations in the museum have disappeared. During the last renovation, however, Cuypers' work was restored to its former glory. The Rijksmuseum was not Cuypers' only showpiece. In 1889, 'his' Central Station in Amsterdam was unveiled. In addition to these famous projects, Cuypers focused primarily on churches and monuments.

CuratorialText_x: Willem Drees (1886-1988) made a career in The Hague as a member of the Social Democratic Workers' Party, which would later become the PvdA. The politician climbed a long ladder of various positions: municipal councilor, alderman, Member of Parliament. In the first cabinet elected after the war, Drees was appointed Minister of Social Affairs. With his Drees Emergency Act, a provision for fixed benefits, he helped the elderly out of financial distress. Drees was popular and became Prime Minister in 1948. It was his task to effectively handle the reconstruction of the Netherlands. Drees proved to be the right man in the right place. With a sense of solidarity, the Netherlands was rebuilt. Under Drees's leadership, until 1958, the foundation of the modern Dutch welfare state was laid. To this day, the statesman is appreciated for his efforts for the Netherlands.

CuratorialText_x: At the end of the 16th century, there was a preference in Italy for Mannerism, a style full of complex compositions and figures in intricate, twisted poses. Around 1600, the Italian Baroque artist Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio broke radically with this art form. His figures are actually very realistic. Wrinkled faces, unkempt hair, dirty feet: gods and saints are depicted as very ordinary people. Caravaggio has a preference for strong light-dark contrasts, dramatic effects, and compositions with a clear structure. His method spreads rapidly throughout Europe. Especially in Utrecht, around 1620, many followers, known as Caravaggists, can be found. Artists such as Gerard van Honthorst, Hendrick ter Brugghen, and Dirck van Baburen travel to Rome to study Caravaggio's style. And although he never visited Italy, Rembrandt was also inspired by Caravaggio's lighting effects.

CuratorialText_x: The 15th and 16th centuries are broadly regarded as the period of the Renaissance, a term that literally means 'rebirth'. In Europe, the arts, literature, and sciences flourish due to a new appreciation of the classical antiquity of the Romans and Greeks. Artists and architects seek ideal proportions. They study Roman architecture and adopt formal languages ​​such as round arches, columns, and pediments. The visual arts increasingly focus on a realistic representation of nature and man, using perspective and anatomical studies. Alongside religious themes, secular themes and mythological representations become popular. The Renaissance spreads from Italy throughout Europe, thanks to the rise of printmaking and printing in the mid-15th century.

persona1_adapted:
The 1400s and 1500s are known as the Renaissance. This word means "rebirth." During this time, art, literature, and science grew quickly in Europe. People began to admire the old cultures of Greece and Rome again.

Artists and architects looked for perfect shapes and sizes in their work. They studied Roman buildings and used features like round arches, columns, and triangular tops called pediments. In painting and sculpture, artists tried to show nature and people in a realistic way. They used perspective, a technique that makes objects look far away or close up. They also studied the human body to make their figures more accurate.

Besides religious stories, artists started painting myths and everyday life. The Renaissance began in Italy and spread across Europe. This happened partly because of new printing methods that appeared around 1450.
persona2_adapted: The 15th and 16th centuries are widely considered the Renaissance period. The term 'Renaissance' literally means 'rebirth.' In Europe, the arts, literature, and sciences flourish. This happens because people develop a new appreciation for the classical antiquity of the Romans and Greeks. Artists and architects seek ideal proportions. They study Roman architecture and adopt formal elements such as round arches, columns, and pediments. The visual arts increasingly focus on a realistic representation of nature and the human figure. They achieve this through the use of perspective and anatomical studies. Alongside religious themes, secular themes and mythological representations become popular. The Renaissance spreads from Italy throughout Europe. This spread is made possible by the rise of printmaking and printing in the mid-15th century.
persona3_adapted: The 15th and 16th centuries are widely known as the Renaissance, a word that literally means "rebirth." During this period, the arts, literature, and sciences thrived across Europe, driven by a renewed interest in the classical cultures of ancient Greece and Rome. Artists and architects sought to create works based on ideal proportions, studying Roman buildings and adopting their distinctive features, such as rounded arches, columns, and triangular roof structures called pediments. In painting and sculpture, there was a growing emphasis on realistic depictions of nature and the human body, achieved through techniques like perspective—which creates the illusion of depth—and careful study of human anatomy. While religious subjects remained important, non-religious themes and stories from classical mythology also became popular. The Renaissance began in Italy and spread throughout Europe, aided by the rise of printmaking and the printing press in the mid-1400s.

CuratorialText_x: The period between the First and Second World Wars is dominated by Art Deco, an international art movement in which tradition and industrial progress go hand in hand. Emerging from Art Nouveau, Art Deco also draws inspiration from stylized plant and floral motifs as well as linear, geometric designs. Additionally, artists look to folk art and art forms from Africa, East Asia, and Central America, a source of inspiration that is further fueled by archaeological finds. The term Art Deco is derived from the 1925 World's Fair in Paris, the Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes.

They also looked at art from Africa, East Asia, and Central America. New archaeological finds made these sources even more popular. The name "Art Deco" comes from a 1925 world's fair in Paris. Artists also look to folk traditions and art forms from Africa, East Asia, and Central America—a source of inspiration further fueled by new archaeological discoveries. The name "Art Deco" comes from the 1925 World's Fair in Paris, officially called the *Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes*.

CuratorialText_x: From around 1150, increasingly tall churches rose in and around Paris, with Notre-Dame as the most famous example. It is seen as the beginning of Gothic architecture, a style that spread rapidly throughout the rest of Europe and would continue until shortly after 1500. Church walls became airier thanks to tall pointed arch windows fitted with colorful stained glass; the vaults rested on slender columns and were strikingly ribbed, and on the exterior, the masonry was supported by slender flying buttresses with pinnacles. Under the influence of the Gothic style, sculptors also gave their human, animal, and plant figures a stylized appearance with elegant poses, calligraphic drapery, and graceful gestures. Meanwhile, the forms of Gothic architecture also seeped into other art forms, for example, painting, illuminated manuscripts, metalwork, and furniture. The Rijksmuseum possesses numerous ecclesiastical objects in this style, such as painted altars, silver monstrances, wooden prayer beads, and a small nativity scene.

persona1_adapted:
Around 1150, churches in and near Paris started to get much taller. The most famous example is Notre-Dame. This marked the beginning of Gothic architecture. The style quickly spread across Europe and lasted until just after 1500.

Church walls became lighter and airier. Tall, pointed arch windows were filled with colorful stained glass. The ceilings rested on thin columns and had striking ribbed patterns. On the outside, the stone walls were supported by slender flying buttresses—arched supports that helped hold up the building's weight. These buttresses often had pointed tops called pinnacles.

Gothic style also influenced sculptors. They gave human, animal, and plant figures a stylized look. The figures had elegant poses, flowing drapery, and graceful gestures.

Gothic shapes and ideas also appeared in other art forms. These included paintings, illuminated manuscripts (hand-decorated books), metalwork, and furniture.

The Rijksmuseum has many church objects in this style. Examples include painted altars, silver monstrances (holders for the holy bread), wooden prayer beads, and a small nativity scene.

CuratorialText_x: The term Impressionism, which means snapshot, was initially used in the early 1870s as a negative label for the work of a group of Parisian painters. Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, and Berthe Morisot depict reality as seen through the eyes of the artist. The French Impressionists are inspired by the realistic style of the French Barbizon School and the work of the slightly older Dutch artist Johan Barthold Jongkind. They work with bright colors and short, loose brushstrokes. They choose their themes from modern life. Due to the sketchy approach, it sometimes appears as if their paintings were created quickly and spontaneously. This often leads critics to say that the artworks are unfinished. From the late 1880s onwards, appreciation for French Impressionism also grows in the Netherlands.

The French Impressionists were inspired by the realistic style of the French Barbizon School. They also looked at the work of the slightly older Dutch artist Johan Barthold Jongkind. persona3_adapted: The term *Impressionism*—which literally means "snapshot"—was first used in the early 1870s as a dismissive label for the work of a group of Parisian painters. Artists like Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, and Berthe Morisot aimed to capture reality as it appeared through the artist's own eyes. These French Impressionists drew inspiration from the realistic style of the earlier French Barbizon School (a group of painters who focused on natural landscapes) and from the work of the slightly older Dutch artist Johan Barthold Jongkind. They used bright colors and applied paint in short, loose strokes, which gave their paintings a sense of movement and immediacy. Their subjects were drawn from modern, everyday life. Because their technique often looked sketchy or unfinished, the paintings could appear as if they had been made quickly and spontaneously. This led many critics at the time to claim the works were incomplete. From the late 1880s onward, however, French Impressionism also began to gain appreciation in the Netherlands.

CuratorialText_x: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.

They wanted something fresh and different. This new style was called Art Nouveau. It used natural shapes like flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The art focused on smooth, flowing lines, simple geometric shapes, and designs that were not perfectly balanced. Art Nouveau started in cities like Paris, Brussels, and Munich. It quickly spread across Western Europe and the United States. This style lasted from about 1890 until the start of World War I in 1914. During that time, Art Nouveau showed up in painting, sculpture, furniture, and many other types of art.

persona3_adapted: At the end of the 19th century, a new artistic style emerged. It was a reaction by artists who rejected the dominant trend of their time—an approach called historicism, which simply means looking backward and imitating the styles of earlier centuries. This fresh style, fittingly named Art Nouveau (French for "new art"), drew inspiration from natural forms like flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. Its key features included flowing, curving lines, geometric shapes, and compositions that were deliberately unbalanced or asymmetrical. With major creative hubs in Paris, Brussels, and Munich, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement across Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from roughly 1890 until the outbreak of World War I in 1914, Art Nouveau made its mark in every field of the arts—from architecture and furniture to painting, jewelry, and graphic design.

CuratorialText_x: Rembrandt is not only a successful painter, but also exceptionally talented as a draftsman and etcher. The Rijksmuseum collection contains approximately 1,300 prints by his hand, including sometimes multiple copies and different states of a single print. The subjects of his etchings range from biblical and mythological scenes to everyday scenes, landscapes, and (self-)portraits. Rembrandt's development in technique, style, and artistic approach can be clearly seen in these works. His loose drawing style and the high-contrast rendering of light and shadow are unique. The choice to depict the most emotional moments is also remarkable for his time. It makes his work human and relatable. Rembrandt has thus had a major influence on artists of his own time and for a long time thereafter.

CuratorialText_x: Under the patronage of Augustus the Strong, Elector of Saxony, alchemist Johann Friedrich Böttger succeeded in becoming the first in Europe to produce porcelain in 1708. Not long afterwards, the first European porcelain factory was established in Meissen. The factory played an important role in the dream of August the Strong: a Japanese Palace, in which he wanted to exhibit his collection of Asian and Meissen porcelain. Since the end of the 16th century, porcelain, also known as 'white gold', had been imported from Asia and collected by the wealthy members of European courts and the elite. The fact that porcelain was now also being produced in Europe is one of the most important moments in the history of the European applied arts. The Rijksmuseum holds one of the most important collections of Meissen porcelain, including pieces from the factory's early production from the collection of Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer.

CuratorialText_x: The Rijksmuseum has been collecting Asian ceramics since its founding in 1885. The largest part of this is Chinese (approx. 2,300 pieces) and Japanese (approx. 600), plus another 50 pieces from other Asian countries. After all, the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia since the founding of the Dutch East India Company (VOC, 1602). From the 17th century onwards, thin-fired porcelain with Chinese blue decorations became immensely popular. Consequently, Chinese and Japanese porcelain play an important role in Dutch interiors. Since 1952, the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK) has also been housed in the Rijksmuseum, comprising pieces that are part of art production in Asia: tea ceramics from Japan, Song bowls and Tang funerary sculptures from China, and ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.

CuratorialText_x: From 1610 onwards, around one hundred thousand pieces of Chinese porcelain were imported into the Netherlands annually. To withstand the Chinese competition, Dutch potters began to refine their products. The earthenware becomes thinner, whiter, and shinier. Blue paintings are applied to a base of white tin glaze, a color scheme strongly associated with Asian porcelain. This earthenware is called 'plateel' or 'faience', but in the 17th and 18th centuries it acquires the name 'Hollants porceleyn'. From 1650 onwards, Delft becomes the center of earthenware production, hence the eventual name 'Delft earthenware'. During the same period, trade in Chinese porcelain comes to a virtual standstill due to the civil war in China (1644-1684). This gives Delft earthenware the opportunity to flourish and gain popularity among the European elite.

This pause gave Delftware a chance to thrive and become highly popular among Europe’s wealthy and powerful classes.

CuratorialText_x: The bronze collection of the Rijksmuseum contains a collection of small sculptures from the Renaissance, primarily from Italy. A number of these originated in the late 15th century in the university city of Padua. These are small bronze utilitarian objects inspired by classical antiquity: candlesticks, perfume burners, oil lamps, and inkwells, all decorated with mythological figures. Many small workshops cast such luxury utensils in series, but unique sculptures and objects were also made in bronze. A good example is the large candlestick that Verrocchio designed in 1469 for Palazzo Vecchio, the city hall of Florence. Bronze casting in the style of classical antiquity also flourished in other Italian cities, such as Rome and Venice. In the 16th and 17th centuries, these figurines found their way into the studies of humanist scholars, art cabinets of wealthy citizens, and Kunstkammern of princes and nobility.

CuratorialText_x: Originally, artists in seascapes primarily captured historical events at sea, so that posterity could relive the heroic deeds. 'War correspondent' and artist Willem van de Velde I created eyewitness paintings of famous Dutch naval battles. His son Willem was a master at capturing calm or, conversely, stormy seas. In the 18th and 19th centuries, Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen opted for a dramatized depiction of ships in distress. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea to study weather effects up close. Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and father and son Schotel, Dutch art abroad became almost synonymous with the seascape. For the artists of the Hague School, the effect of light and cloudy skies played a major role, and they paid more attention to the harsh life of fishermen.

His son, also named Willem, was a master at painting calm seas—or stormy ones. In the 1700s and 1800s, artists like Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen chose to show ships in danger in a dramatic way. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea to study weather effects up close.

Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and the father-and-son team Schotel, Dutch art became almost the same as seascapes in other countries. persona3_adapted: Originally, artists who painted the sea focused mainly on recording important naval battles, so that future generations could experience those heroic moments. Willem van de Velde I, who worked like a modern war correspondent, created firsthand paintings of famous Dutch naval conflicts. His son, Willem van de Velde II, was especially skilled at depicting either perfectly calm waters or, on the other hand, violent storms. In the 1700s and 1800s, artists like Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen chose to portray ships in danger in a more dramatic, emotionally charged way. For the artists of the Hague School—a group known for their realistic, atmospheric style—the way light played across the sky and the shifting cloud patterns were central concerns. They also paid more attention to the harsh, difficult lives of fishermen.

CuratorialText_x: In the 17th century, the marksmen of the civic guard were regularly immortalized in group portraits. At that time, the powerful cities themselves were responsible for security. The militia ensured the maintenance of order and security. Every adult man up to the age of 60 is expected to join, although he must be able to purchase his own weaponry. The group portraits are a costly undertaking, as each marksman pays for his own portrait. The earliest marksman portraits date from the 16th century and are rather static. In the course of the 17th century, painters opted for a somewhat looser composition, with Rembrandt's Night Watch and the lively Banquet of the Marksmen by Bartholomeus van der Helst as highlights. Not only marksmen's guilds commission group portraits; other urban, provincial, and national organizations and individuals also commission them.

CuratorialText_x: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.

Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has about 230 still life paintings. A still life is a type of art that shows objects arranged together. Flemish painters started making these in the 1500s.

These paintings often show things like shells, books, food, drinks, dishes, skulls, or dead animals. They can also include flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. In a still life, everything is frozen in time.

Soon, artists began to focus on different subjects. For example, Rachel Ruysch mostly painted flowers. Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda liked to paint food, cups, and plates.

Another type is the vanitas still life. It often shows a skull. This reminds viewers that life and material things do not last forever.

In these paintings, life literally stands still for a moment.

Soon, separate specializations developed within the genre. For example, Rachel Ruysch focused primarily on flowers. CuratorialText_x: The Rijksmuseum has an enormous collection of portraits from the late Middle Ages to the present day. In addition to their artistic value, these more than 1,700 portraits give the past a face. They describe what people looked like: what clothing and jewelry they wore, what their hairstyles were like, and sometimes what the interiors looked like at the time. There are also significant differences, as each century emphasizes different aspects. In the Renaissance, portraits seem to become increasingly realistic, whereas artists from the 20th and 21st centuries move away from that realism. Throughout the centuries, governments, organizations, and individuals have commissioned portraits for all kinds of occasions: to confirm profession or status, for a wedding, or as a memorial after someone's death. The Rijksmuseum collection also contains many self-portraits by famous painters such as Jan Steen, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh.

CuratorialText_x: Genre paintings are paintings depicting scenes from everyday life. They are lively scenes that often take place in kitchens, living rooms, inns, and markets. They capture people in their natural environment: children romping, a family huddled together in the kitchen, or a merchant hawking his wares. The first genre paintings emerged in 16th-century Flemish painting, but in the Netherlands, genre art did not flourish until a century later. The works of Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster became famous. They often concealed a mischievous content or, conversely, a moral message in their work. The term 'genre paintings' did not emerge until the 19th century, when the popularity of genre art reached a peak. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes works by 19th-century painters such as Jozef Israëls, known for his lively scenes of farmers and fishermen.

CuratorialText_x: Water is crucial for the development of the Netherlands as a trading nation. The economy grows thanks to the raw materials that enter via rivers and the world's oceans. Consider bulk goods such as grain and timber from the Baltic region, wool from England, wine from France, and raw salt from the Iberian Peninsula. Using these raw materials, the Dutch produce items such as beer, cloth (woolen fabric), fine salt (for the herring trade), weapons, and ships. Many of these products are subsequently exported, often again by water. Cities are important links in this international trade. In the 15th and 16th centuries, Antwerp is the largest port city of the Netherlands. After the (re)conquest by the Spaniards during the Dutch Revolt, Holland, and especially Amsterdam, takes over the role as a staple market. International trade leads to the emergence of a financial market, significant employment, and strong urban growth.

CuratorialText_x: What is money actually worth? Why does a small piece of metal worth a few euro cents suddenly have a much higher value due to the '2 euro' imprint? Whoever holds the right to mint coins chooses the appearance, the amount of precious metal, and the value. Through trade contacts, gold and silver coins spread across the world for centuries. Coins with little gold or silver circulated primarily locally, because once abroad, the metal value was of paramount importance. The Amsterdam Exchange Bank was for a long time a key hub for the trade in precious metals. As early as the Eighty Years' War, a large portion of the silver that Spain obtained from Spanish America ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 18th century, veritable silver fleets sailed from Spain to the Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled the world, such as to Asia via the VOC.

**What is money actually worth?**

Why does a small piece of metal worth a few cents suddenly have a much higher value just because it has "2 euro" stamped on it? The person or group that has the right to make coins gets to choose how they look, how much precious metal they contain, and what they are worth.

For centuries, gold and silver coins spread around the world through trade. Coins that had little gold or silver mostly stayed in local areas. That's because when they traveled to other countries, people cared most about the metal they were made of.

For a long time, the Amsterdam Exchange Bank was a key center for trading precious metals. As early as the Eighty Years' War, a large amount of the silver that Spain took from the Americas ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 1700s, huge silver fleets sailed from Spain to the Dutch Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled all over the world—for example, to Asia with the Dutch East India Company (VOC).

The authority to mint coins determines their design, the amount of precious metal they contain, and their assigned worth. For centuries, gold and silver coins spread across the globe through trade. Coins with only a small amount of gold or silver tended to circulate mainly within local regions, because once they traveled abroad, the actual metal content became the key measure of value. The Amsterdam Exchange Bank long served as a major center for the trade in precious metals. Until well into the 1700s, regular fleets of silver ships sailed from Spain to the Dutch Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled onward across the world—for example, to Asia through the Dutch East India Company (VOC).

CuratorialText_x: William of Orange is regarded as the 'Father of the Fatherland' because of his leading role in the struggle for independence. The House of Orange owes its princely title to the insignificant French city-state of Orange on the Rhône, yet manages to carve out a place for itself among the great European royal houses through its wealth and prestige. The wives of the stadtholders play an important role in these networks. Nevertheless, it seems to be the end for the House of Orange at the end of the 18th century: Stadtholder William V flees to England, and the Batavian Republic is proclaimed. But in 1813, his eldest son returns and becomes King William I. From that moment on, the Netherlands is a 'constitutional monarchy,' and a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always sat on the throne.

CuratorialText_x: Whose object was it before it came to the museum? The lives, relationships, and circumstances of the owners tell something about the significance of the object. Provenance research reveals stories of pre-war owners and reminds us of the people who were persecuted during the Holocaust. Even if objects were not looted, their provenance provides insight into their history. The Rijksmuseum collection primarily tells the stories of collectors from the period between 1933 and 1945. Research into the collection can therefore not possibly provide a complete picture of all persecuted owners. After all, many everyday objects did not end up in museums or are more difficult to identify. Nevertheless, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us of the diversity of pre-war Europe. Provenance research shows that the people persecuted by the Nazis actively contributed to Dutch culture and history. Objects in museums are still the silent witnesses of this today.

CuratorialText_x: Before the Europeans arrive, centuries-old trade routes already exist around the Indian Ocean. The Dutch East India Company (VOC) forcibly expands these networks and exploits areas such as the Moluccas and Sri Lanka for spices. Batavia (Jakarta) and the Cape of Good Hope become important centers for sugar, coffee, and rice. In the Atlantic region, Dutch merchants trade in products such as sugar and coffee, which leads to the establishment of the Dutch West India Company (WIC). Over time, slave labor and the slave trade take place at all trading posts. Even for a craftsman in the Netherlands, there is money to be made from colonial trade, for example as a supplier of products for the slave trade or for daily life in the colonies. The transport of the products to Europe and their further processing also generates revenue. In this way, colonial trade stimulates the national economy.

CuratorialText_x: The Netherlands was a colonial power for more than 350 years and established trading posts and colonies worldwide in the 17th century, including in Indonesia, South Africa, Sri Lanka, South America, and the Caribbean. Dutch colonial settlement often took place through the conclusion of trade contracts, but also through military force. The Rijksmuseum possesses many (art) objects that were brought to the Netherlands during and after this period, ranging from war booty to items acquired through trade. It is important to be open about the history of this collection and to reflect on the role of the Netherlands in the colonial past. Through research and collaboration with countries of origin, the Rijksmuseum aims to provide a nuanced and diverse picture of this shared past.

CuratorialText_x: For 750 years, Amsterdam has been a city that grows, flourishes, and buzzes. Residents from all corners of the world have made the city what it is today. The first inhabitants of Amsterdam were farmers and fishermen, living along the River Amstel. In the late Middle Ages, Amsterdam grew into a small port city amidst the peatlands. Partly due to the establishment of the trading companies VOC and WIC, the city transformed rapidly into a global metropolis with more than 200,000 inhabitants during the 17th century, where almost every Amsterdammer was directly or indirectly involved in colonial trade. From rope makers to cartographers, from notaries to confectioners. To manage the population growth, a plan was drawn up to expand the city. It was the beginning of the famous canal belt.

CuratorialText_x: The tulip originates from Turkey and was introduced to Europe in the late 16th century. The flower proved to thrive particularly well in the sandy soil of the Dutch coastal region. In the 17th century, traders of tulips and other exotic flowers began publishing 'Florilegia', books with detailed illustrations serving as catalogues of their wares. Enthusiasts of unusual flowers and plants could also turn to painters and draftsmen. Flower still lifes became a popular genre in art. Real flowers wilt, but paintings could serve as durable substitutes. Moreover, these flower still lifes sometimes cost even less than the flowers depicted on the canvas.

CuratorialText_x: Throughout history, the role of pets changes significantly, from useful working animals to beloved companions. It was not until between 500 and 1500 that noblemen began keeping dogs for their company. In paintings from the 17th century, these four-legged creatures often appear in that role. For a long time, cats were primarily used as mouse exterminators and had a bad reputation due to their medieval association with witchcraft. It was not until the Victorian era that people accepted cats as pets. A beautiful pedigree cat, such as a British Shorthair or the Persian Longhair, became a status symbol for wealthy citizens. From the 19th century onwards, dogs and cats, and later birds and fish, became everyday pets for all social classes. Artist Henriëtte Ronner-Knip (1821–1909) specialized in painting cats, which brought her fame. She was regularly commissioned to immortalize the beloved pets of aristocratic and bourgeois families.

CuratorialText_x: Sculpture. From the Middle Ages to the present day, in every shape and size and encompassing a wide range of materials. This expanding online catalog presents a comprehensive overview of the latest scholarly insights into the Rijksmuseum's vast and highly diverse collection of European sculpture. This online catalog includes every work of European sculpture in the Rijksmuseum's art collection. The most recently published catalog of sculpture dates from 1973. Prompting a thorough update of this valuable, but by now outdated reference work was the ever-growing body of art historical research and the long list of acquisitions made in recent decades.   The Rijksmuseum (and its precursors: the Royal Cabinet of Rareities and later the Dutch Museum of History and Art) began acquiring sculpture on a systematic basis only in the last quarter of the nineteenth century. Additions to the collection prior to this time are few in number. One early example is the Dutch government's purchase ofArtus Quellinus's magnificent marble bust portrait of Andries de Graeff in 1817. Initially, the museum's sculpture acquisition policy was primarily oriented to works of national interest. It was not until the twentieth century that this focus gradually began to shift, largely thanks to the efforts of Adriaan Pit (museum director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (curator of sculpture from 1948 to 1969 and compiler of the 1973 catalogue), who sought to actively acquire international works. As a result, sculptures of non-Netherlandish origin today make up approximately half of the collection. This international scope received an additional impulse when, in 1952, the Dutch government charged the Rijksmuseum with the care of Italian and German works formerly in the possession of Fritz Mannheimer and recovered from Germany after the Second World War. This enhancement of the collection has since served to stimulate the museum's policy of acquiring international works.

persona1_adapted:
**Sculpture at the Rijksmuseum**

Sculpture comes in all shapes and sizes. It can be made from many different materials. This online catalog shows you the Rijksmuseum's large and varied collection of European sculpture. It includes every European sculpture in the museum's art collection.

The last printed catalog of sculpture came out in 1973. That book is now outdated. Art historians have learned a lot since then. The museum has also added many new sculptures. So it was time for a thorough update.

The Rijksmuseum (and its earlier versions: the Royal Cabinet of Rareities and the Dutch Museum of History and Art) began collecting sculpture on a regular basis only in the late 1800s. Before that, the museum added very few sculptures. One early example is a marble bust of Andries de Graeff. The Dutch government bought it in 1817. It was made by the artist Artus Quellinus.

At first, the museum focused on buying sculptures that were important to the Netherlands. That changed in the 1900s. Two people helped make this change happen. Adriaan Pit was museum director from 1898 to 1917. Jaap Leeuwenberg was curator of sculpture from 1948 to 1969. He also put together the 1973 catalog. They worked to buy sculptures from other countries.

Today, about half of the collection comes from outside the Netherlands. This international focus grew even more in 1952. That year, the Dutch government asked the Rijksmuseum to take care of Italian and German sculptures. These works had belonged to Fritz Mannheimer. They were recovered from Germany after World War II. This addition helped the museum keep buying international works.

persona3_adapted:
**Sculpture at the Rijksmuseum: A Living Collection**

From the Middle Ages to today, sculpture has taken countless forms and sizes, made from an enormous variety of materials. This growing online catalog offers a broad, up-to-date look at what scholars now know about the Rijksmuseum's vast and remarkably diverse collection of European sculpture. It includes every European sculpture in the museum's art collection.

The last printed catalog of the museum's sculptures was published in 1973. That valuable reference work has since become outdated, mainly because art-historical research has expanded greatly and the museum has added many new works over the past few decades. This online catalog is a thorough update.

The Rijksmuseum (and its earlier versions—the Royal Cabinet of Curiosities and later the Dutch Museum of History and Art) only began collecting sculpture in a planned, systematic way during the last quarter of the nineteenth century. Before that, the museum acquired very few sculptures. One early exception was the Dutch government's purchase in 1817 of a magnificent marble bust of Andries de Graeff, carved by the artist Artus Quellinus.

At first, the museum focused its sculpture collecting on works that were important to Dutch national history and culture. It wasn't until the twentieth century that this focus gradually shifted, largely thanks to two key figures: Adriaan Pit (museum director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (curator of sculpture from 1948 to 1969, who also compiled the 1973 catalog). Both actively sought to acquire sculptures from outside the Netherlands. As a result, about half of the collection today comes from other European countries.

This international direction received another major boost in 1952, when the Dutch government asked the Rijksmuseum to take care of Italian and German sculptures that had once belonged to Fritz Mannheimer. These works had been recovered from Germany after World War II. This addition encouraged the museum to continue its policy of acquiring international sculptures.

CuratorialText_x:
The Rijksmuseum holds a world-class collection of Japanese works on paper: woodblock prints, illustrated books and drawings. Among the almost 4000 prints, more than 800 are exclusive, privately published surimono. This online catalog features 203 surimono donated to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by the collector JHW Goslings (1943-2011). The entries in this catalog are taken from Matthi Forrer's 2013 collection catalog Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam.
Poetry and image combined
“Surimono (literally 'printed things') constitute one of the most delicate genres in Japanese printmaking. This genre fascinates because it combines poetry and image and because it presents a pictorial puzzle, which provides the viewer with a particular insight into the intellectual and literary world of the late 18th- and early 19th-century Edo (today's Tokyo). Major artists such asKatsushika Hokusai,Utagawa Kunisada,Toyota HokkeiandYashima Gakutei, to name but a few, provided imagery to accompany the poetic exploits of poetry club members.

CuratorialText_x: He sits with outstretched wings on a cloud placed on a round plinth, holding his right index finger to the mouth of his mischievously smiling face, while reaching with his left hand for an arrow in the quiver lying beside him. His left leg is drawn up, and the foot of his right leg, positioned diagonally in front of him, extends over the round plinth. Beside him lies a flowering rose branch. The quiver filled with arrows displays, among other things, fluting, a wreath of laurel leaves, and godrons. The pedestal, a short column, has a profiled base decorated with a wreath of laurel leaves, a shaft with curved fluting containing culots transitioning into a smooth upper section interrupted by a profiled edge, and furthermore, a profiled, overhanging cover plate decorated with egg-and-dart molding, which is rotatable by means of two brass knobs.

CuratorialText_x: This portrait of Alida Assink presents her in her finest attire, depicted from head to toe. That is quite exceptional, as the painter Kruseman typically reserved such a full-length portrait exclusively for royalty. Assink was 23 years old when this likeness was commissioned by her guardian, a wealthy landowner. The rural setting with hunting dog and garden vase harks back to the English portrait style, which Kruseman admired.

Medium: watercolor, over a very light pencil sketch, outline in brown ink, brush in watercolor in colors
CuratorialText_x: The Dordrecht artist Aert Schouman was no scientist, but he was deeply interested in true-to-life depictions of the bird realm. His keen powers of observation are demonstrated by the way in which this red-billed toucan grasps the branch, namely with two, rather than three toes like other species. The landscape airily painted in transparent shades of green and blue evokes the bird’s native regions in Guyana and North Brazil.
CuratorialText_y: The Dordrecht artist Aert Schouman was no scientist, but he was deeply interested in true-to-life depictions of the bird realm. His keen powers of observation are demonstrated by the way in which this red-billed toucan grasps the branch, namely with two, rather than three toes like other species. The landscape airily painted in transparent shades of green and blue evokes the bird’s native regions in Guyana and North Brazil.
persona1_adapted: Aert Schouman was an artist from Dordrecht. He was not a scientist, but he cared a lot about painting birds that looked real. His sharp observation skills show in how this red-billed toucan holds the branch. It uses two toes, not three like other birds. The background is painted lightly in see-through greens and blues. It reminds us of the bird’s home in Guyana and North Brazil.
persona2_adapted: The Dordrecht artist Aert Schouman was not a scientist, but he had a strong interest in realistic depictions of birds. The landscape, painted in a light, airy style with transparent shades of green and blue, suggests the bird’s native regions in Guyana and North Brazil.

The landscape, painted in light, see-through shades of green and blue, suggests the bird’s natural home in Guyana and northern Brazil.

CuratorialText_x: Here the Duke of Wellington is receiving the message that Prussian forces are coming to his aid. Wellington, commander of the Anglo-Dutch troops, is the central figure in this group portrait of the major players at Waterloo. Lying wounded on a stretcher in the left foreground is the Dutch Crown Prince, later King William II. Originally intended for Wellington, the painting remained in the Netherlands thanks to William I who bought the picture for his son.

NameOfTheObject: Still Life of Fruit and Flowers
Dimension: support: height 39.2 cm x width 69.8 cm
CuratorialText_x: This still life invites close examination of all the details and elicits marvel at the richness of God’s creation: the colourful flowers, the exotic shells, but certainly also the beautiful insects. At the same time, decay is spreading. Fruit shows the first rotten spots, leaves are eaten away by caterpillars and snails and ants drag a dead fly. The painting is thus also about impermanence.

CuratorialText_x: Presented here are two squirrel monkeys from Central America, two white sulphur-crested cockatoos from Australia, a grey parrot from Africa and a purple-naped lory – on a chain at the lower left – from Indonesia. Hondecoeter combined these creatures and several other splendid birds in this painting, which was destined for Het Loo, the palace of William III. It hung above the door of the king’s private apartment.

NameOfTheObject: Banquet at the Crossbowmen’s Guild in Celebration of the Treaty of Münster
CuratorialText_x: 18 June 1648: a banquet is taking place at the Amsterdam crossbowmen’s guild. The occasion was the signing of the Treaty of Münster, which marked an end to the war with Spain. The captains of the civic guard company shake hands as a sign of peace, and the drinking horn is passed around. The poem on the drum proclaims the joy of Amsterdam’s armed militia that their weapons can henceforth be laid to rest.

The poem on the drum proclaims the joy of Amsterdam’s armed militia that their weapons can henceforth be laid to rest.

The captains of the city's guard shake hands as a sign of peace. They pass around a drinking horn. A poem on the drum announces the joy of Amsterdam's armed militia. Their weapons can now be put to rest.

support: height 98.5 cm x width 187.8 cm
sight size: height 97 cm x width 186.7 cm
CuratorialText_x: The Fishing for Souls: an allegory of the jealousy of the various religions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609-1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain. In a river landscape, large groups of people are situated on both banks of the river; several rowboats are on the water. On the horizon, a rainbow. On the left side are the Protestants and their supporters: the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry, Frederick V of the Palatinate, James I, King of England, and the youthful Louis XIII, King of France, with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat in the foreground sit the Protestant fishermen; their nets are marked with Fides, Spes, and Charitas. On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope carried by cardinals. In the small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the leadership of a bishop.

**Adapted Text:**

*Fishing for Souls* is an allegory. It represents the jealousy between different religions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609–1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain.

In a river landscape, large groups of people stand on both banks. Several rowboats are on the water. A rainbow appears on the horizon.

On the left side are the Protestants and their supporters: the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry; Frederick V of the Palatinate; James I, King of England; and the young Louis XIII, King of France, with his mother, Maria de' Medici.

In the boat in the foreground, the Protestant fishermen sit. Their nets are marked with the words *Fides*, *Spes*, and *Charitas* (Faith, Hope, and Charity).

In the small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the leadership of a bishop.
persona3_adapted:
**Fishing for Souls: An Allegory of Religious Rivalry During the Twelve Years' Truce (1609–1621)**

This painting is an allegory—a symbolic story—about the intense competition between different Christian groups during a temporary peace between the Dutch Republic and Spain. The scene unfolds across a river landscape, with large crowds gathered on both banks and several rowboats on the water. A rainbow stretches across the horizon.

On the left side are the Protestants and their allies. These include the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry; Frederick V of the Palatinate; James I, King of England; and the young Louis XIII, King of France, with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat closest to us, Protestant fishermen are at work. Their nets are marked with the Latin words *Fides*, *Spes*, and *Charitas*—meaning Faith, Hope, and Charity, three central Christian virtues.

On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope, who is being carried by cardinals. In the smaller Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the direction of a bishop.

The painting uses the act of fishing as a metaphor for each side's efforts to win followers for their faith. The river itself represents the divide between the two groups, and the rainbow may suggest hope or divine promise—though the scene is charged with tension, not harmony.

NameOfTheObject: Portrait of Marie Jeanette de Lange
CuratorialText_x: The painting is executed in a pointillist technique. The sitter is positioned on the right, facing the viewer, while studying a book in the light of a kerosene lamp. She is dressed in a pink gown, known as reform clothing, and has her hair simply styled in an updo. The kerosene lamp is partially visible on the table on the left, placed next to a small vase of roses on a small table covered with a floral tablecloth. Flowers in a large vase in the background are visually incorporated into the pattern of the decorative wallpaper.
CuratorialText_y: The painting is executed in a pointillist technique. The sitter is positioned on the right, facing the viewer, while studying a book in the light of a kerosene lamp. persona3_adapted: The painting uses a technique called pointillism, where the artist builds the image from many tiny dots of color rather than brushstrokes. The woman sits on the right side of the canvas, facing us as she reads a book by the light of a kerosene lamp. She wears a pink gown—a style known as reform clothing, which was part of a movement for more practical and comfortable women's fashion—and her hair is simply pinned up. The lamp is partly visible on the table to the left, next to a small vase of roses on a table covered with a floral cloth. In the background, flowers in a large vase blend into the pattern of the decorative wallpaper, so that the real and painted flowers seem to merge.

NameOfTheObject: Dutch Ships in a Calm Sea
CuratorialText_x: Six warships lie on a vast, calm sea. The sails are being hoisted, the anchors weighed. The stern of the ship on the right in the foreground is decorated with the coat of arms of Amsterdam, supported by two standing lions. This likely depicts part of a war fleet preparing for departure. A sloop with dignified figures, probably from the Admiralty, sails past the ships to the sound of trumpets and salutes, while a little further away the last provisions and crew are still being taken on board. The enormous accuracy in the rendering of details—rigging, sails, and cannons—is striking.

NameOfTheObject: Giuliano & Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo Piero di Cosimo (1462–1522)
CuratorialText_x: Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445-1516). Architect, son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. Bust facing right, with a landscape in the background. A compass and pen lie on the plinth at the bottom of the image. Part, with SK-C-1367, of a diptych.
CuratorialText_y: Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445-1516). Architect, son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. Bust facing right, with a landscape in the background. A compass and pen lie on the plinth at the bottom of the image. Part, with SK-C-1367, of a diptych.

persona3_adapted:
**Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445–1516)**  
This portrait shows Giuliano da Sangallo, an architect and the son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. He is depicted from the chest up, facing to the right, with a landscape visible behind him. Some, such as alms bowls, are linked overtly to dervishes, while others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphic works, miniature paintings and wall tiles. CuratorialText_x: Hawaiʻi: a kingdom crossing oceans is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. The exhibition brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations. Building on years of collaboration with Native Hawaiian artists, practitioners and scholars, the show centres on indigenous knowledge, shining new light on exceptional objects and extraordinary stories. Shaped together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the exhibition showcases seldom-seen international loans alongside the remarkable collections at the British Museum – one of the largest in the world outside of Hawaiʻi.

CuratorialText_x: Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006) on the theme of revolution and its relationship to contemporary society. Finlay became known from the late 1950s for his poetry but in the 1960s his output expanded to include the production of prints and three-dimensional works of art in stone, metal, neon and glass, which he produced in collaboration with various craftspeople. He also became a dedicated gardener, developing a conceptual garden at his remote home on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh, which incorporated many of his works of art. He named his garden Little Sparta after the ancient Greek city, a militaristic rival to Athens, reflecting his deep interest in classical history.

CuratorialText_x: In the 1980s, Finlay's interest in the French Revolution began to dominate his work. He was fascinated by revolutions and ruptures, and how high ideals could easily lead to violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display, made between 1984 and 1992, form part of Finlay's engagement with the French Revolution as a subject. They are from a group of 26 prints donated in 2025 by Prof Stephen Bann, an art historian who has written extensively on Finlay and who corresponded with the artist for many years.

CuratorialText_x: In a career that lasted from the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) experienced some of the most turbulent events in French history. A pupil of Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805), Wille established himself by imitating the genre scenes that won Greuze renown at the Salons of the 1760s, showing an inclination for delicacy that suited the tastes of the ancien régime.

Wille studied under Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805). He built his career by copying the everyday life scenes that made Greuze famous at the art shows of the 1760s. Wille’s work showed a taste for fine details, which fit the style that people liked during the old French monarchy, known as the *ancien régime*.

persona3_adapted: In a career that spanned the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) lived through some of the most dramatic upheavals in French history. Trained by the well-known painter Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805), Wille first gained recognition by creating scenes of everyday life—a style known as "genre scenes"—that had made Greuze famous at the official art exhibitions called the Salons in the 1760s. Wille’s work showed a preference for gentle, refined details, which matched the fashionable tastes of France’s old ruling order, the ancien régime.

CuratorialText_x: Yet Wille the man embraced the Revolution, even joining the republican army, and he busied himself chronicling the personalities of the day. Ultimately, however, the Revolution proved more of a challenge than an opportunity. The younger Wille had embraced a variety of styles but, in later life, he found it difficult to adapt to the more austere taste of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had long been reduced to poverty. During his lifetime and since, Wille was renowned more for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been acquiring his work since the 19th century, and its collection has recently doubled in size following a bequest from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of the finest finished drawings by Wille; one of the most powerful is the portrait which claims to show the 'Angel of Assassination' Charlotte Corday, made in her prison cell days or even hours before her execution. This celebration of Clark's bequest illustrates Wille's world through his drawings and those of his peers, revealing him to have been a talented draughtsman whose works offer a fascinating insight into French society before, during and after the Revolution.

CuratorialText_x: Made in England between 1340 and 1405, the Asante Ewer later travelled to West Africa. In the 1880s it was photographed in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene, king of the Asante people, in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896 it was looted by British forces and subsequently purchased by the British Museum. A second ewer, also taken in 1896 and shown in the same photograph, was presented to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This display reunites these two jugs. A major new research project into the Asante Ewer – one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze casting – is helping to further trace the story of its journey to Ghana, and its status and significance in West Africa. Its location in the centre of the palace courtyard, in what appears to be a shrine, embedded in the roots of sacred trees, suggests it was an object of significant cultural or ritual value. Remarkable for surviving the long-distance journey from Europe to West Africa and back, the Asante Ewer has had vastly different lives, from a luxury English jug to sacred West African vessel, to colonial loot taken by British troops.

CuratorialText_x: In the 1400s and 1500s, the Low Countries were a powerhouse of artistic innovation. This was a transformative period for Netherlandish drawings, as they evolved from preparatory studies to works of art in their own right.

persona1_adapted: In the 1400s and 1500s, the Low Countries were a major center for new art ideas. During this time, drawings in the Netherlands changed a lot. They started as rough sketches to plan other artworks. Later, they became finished pieces of art that people collected and admired.
persona2_adapted: In the 1400s and 1500s, the Low Countries were a major center of artistic innovation. During this transformative period, Netherlandish drawings evolved from preparatory studies into independent works of art.
persona3_adapted: In the 1400s and 1500s, the region now known as the Low Countries (roughly modern-day Belgium, Netherlands, and Luxembourg) was a major center of artistic creativity. During this time, drawings in this area underwent a significant shift: they were no longer just rough sketches made in preparation for paintings or other finished works, but began to be valued as independent pieces of art in their own right.

CuratorialText_x: Featuring around 120 works by artists including Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder and Hendrick Goltzius, this exhibition charts the development of drawing in the Low Countries (present-day Belgium, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) before 1600. The exceptional breadth and quality of the British Museum's holdings provides a unique opportunity to present a comprehensive account of drawing from this region.  Early Netherlandish drawings are extremely rare, as they were made as functional objects in the workshop and were not typically retained. This was unlike Italy or the German-speaking lands, where the taste for collecting drawings in the 1500s ensured the survival of a higher number of works from this period. Drawings were central to the design and production of works of art in different media, including tapestries, paintings, stained glass and prints – and surviving examples shed light on the creativity and collaboration of Netherlandish artists.

The British Museum has a very large and high-quality collection of these drawings. This gives us a rare chance to tell a full story about drawing in this region.

CuratorialText_x: Based on a research project and supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, the exhibition highlights new findings that have come from combining curatorial and conservation expertise along with scientific analysis. New research into drawings previously only connected to Rogier van der Weyden and his circle, for instance, reveals they originate directly from Rogier's workshop and must have been drawn by his pupils as part of their training.

CuratorialText_x: Featuring a 10ft bronze statue of Elizabeth as a young Queen, on a 14ft plinth, the memorial in St James' Park will commemorate the late monarch and her lifelong commitment to public service. The memorial is expected to be completed in 2028. Designed by acclaimed sculptor Martin Jennings, to be sculpted in bronze, the statue will show Her Majesty in an early stage of her reign, dressed in the robes of the Order of the Garter, using a famous painting by Pietro Annigoni as inspiration.

A new memorial in St James’s Park will honor Queen Elizabeth II and her lifelong dedication to public service. It features a 10-foot bronze statue of the Queen as a young monarch, standing on a 14-foot base called a plinth. The memorial is expected to be finished in 2028.

The statue was designed by famous sculptor Martin Jennings. It will be made of bronze and shows the Queen early in her reign. She wears the robes of the Order of the Garter, a special honor. The design was inspired by a well-known painting by Pietro Annigoni.

persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

The memorial in St James' Park will feature a 10-foot bronze statue of Queen Elizabeth as a young monarch, standing atop a 14-foot base. Designed by the celebrated sculptor Martin Jennings, the statue is expected to be completed by 2028. It will depict the Queen early in her reign, wearing the ceremonial robes of the Order of the Garter—a prestigious British knighthood order. Jennings drew inspiration from a well-known portrait of the Queen painted by the Italian artist Pietro Annigoni. The memorial is intended to honor the late Queen and her lifelong dedication to public service.

CuratorialText_x: Mr Jennings will also sculpt a statue of Prince Philip, the country's longest-serving consort, at a similar age wearing a uniform of Admiral-of-the-Fleet. His statue will be located close to Queen Elizabeth's, in recognition of the support he gave her during her 70-year reign. Other elements of the memorial have been designed by Foster + Partners in collaboration with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The British Museum display features maquettes of the statues and a scale model of the wider memorial park. The King and Queen, other Members of the Royal Family and the Prime Minister viewed the designs at the Museum on 21 April to mark the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth. The permanent memorial will also feature: a new cast-glass bridge inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara, which was worn by Queen Elizabeth on her wedding day; a bust of Her Majesty in her later years by sculptor Karen Newman; and The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract work by Yinka Shonibare.

CuratorialText_x:
Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt and explore their hopes and aspirations for the afterlife. 
From human remains, painted coffins, amulets, shabtis and tomb models of daily life to spectacular wall paintings and papyri; investigate what tombs, human remains and burial goods tell us about the lives and afterlives of ancient Egyptian people. The tour will explore the preparation of the deceased for burial, including mummification, the furnishing of the tomb for an eternal restful existence, and the use of magic to guide and protect people on their perilous journey to the afterlife. It will also show how funerary remains can tell us about the lives of the ancient Egyptians. This tour visits displays that include human remains. The British Museum is committed to curating human remains with care, respect and dignity. Find out more about our principles governing the holding, display and study of human remains.

The tour examines human remains, painted coffins, amulets, shabtis, and tomb models of daily life. It also includes spectacular wall paintings and papyri. Together, these objects help us understand what tombs, human remains, and burial goods reveal about the lives and afterlives of ancient Egyptian people.

The tour will explore several key topics:
- The preparation of the deceased for burial, including mummification.
- The furnishing of the tomb for an eternal, restful existence.
- The use of magic to guide and protect people on their dangerous journey to the afterlife.

The tour also shows how funerary remains can tell us about the lives of the ancient Egyptians.

Please note: This tour visits displays that include human remains. The British Museum is committed to curating human remains with care, respect, and dignity. You can find more information about our principles governing the holding, display, and study of human remains.

Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt, and learn about their beliefs and dreams for what happens after death. 

Through human remains, painted coffins, protective charms, small servant figurines called *shabtis*, and miniature models of everyday life—alongside stunning wall paintings and ancient scrolls made from papyrus—this tour explores what tombs, bodies, and burial objects reveal about the lives and afterlives of ancient Egyptians. 

You’ll see how the dead were prepared for burial, including the process of mummification (preserving the body for the next world), how tombs were furnished for an eternal, peaceful existence, and how magical practices were used to guide and protect people on their dangerous journey to the afterlife. The tour also shows how these burial remains can tell us about daily life in ancient Egypt. 

Please note that this tour includes displays of human remains. The British Museum is committed to treating these remains with care, respect, and dignity. You can learn more about our principles for holding, displaying, and studying human remains.

CuratorialText_x:
Discover the fascinating history of ancient Egypt and explore one of the most iconic spaces in the Museum before it opens to the public.
In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you can see impressive statues of kings and gods, monumental tomb architecture and ancient tomb reliefs spanning 3,000 year. These include the imposing colossal bust of Ramesses II and the Gayer-Anderson Cat. Learn about the importance of large-scale sculpture in ancient Egyptian temples, tombs and much more in this special guided tour.

CuratorialText_x: Founded in 1753, the British Museum opened its doors to visitors in 1759. The Museum tells the story of human cultural achievement through a collection of collections. This room celebrates some of the collectors who, in different ways, have shaped the Museum over four centuries, along with individuals and organisations who continue to shape its future. The adjoining galleries also explore aspects of collecting. Room 1: Enlightenment, tells the story of how, in the early Museum, objects and knowledge were gathered and classified. Room 2a: The Waddesdon Bequest displays the collection of Renaissance and Baroque masterpieces left to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild MP at his death in 1898.

CuratorialText_x: The British Museum has successfully raised £3.5 million, which means the Tudor Heart Pendant will remain on public display for generations to come.  In December 2019 a metal detectorist in Warwickshire found a spectacular Tudor Heart jewel associated with Henry VIII and his first wife Katherine of Aragon in the ground after centuries of rest. The Tudor Heart Pendant is unlike any object in the British Museum collection or elsewhere in the UK. This highly accomplished jewel with enamelled motifs is attached to a 75-link gold chain by a suspension clasp in the form of a hand emerging from a cloud. Few objects survive to tell the story of Henry's life with the devoutly Catholic Katherine, a consequence of the annulment of their marriage in 1533. Their union was the longest of all of Henry's, lasting 24 years it decisively shaped the first three decades of his reign.

CuratorialText_x: In the 18th century, the collections were divided into 'Natural and Artificial Rarities' – objects found in nature or made by people. Only a few rooms in the early Museum had manmade objects but case after case was filled with natural specimens from Sloane's collection and his library which included the 265 volumes of his herbarium. In 1735, the Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus revolutionised the way plants, animals and other objects from the natural world were named and classified. CuratorialText_x: Antiquaries collected books, manuscripts, drawings and artefacts from the past, and travelled the country studying ruins in order to learn more about Britain's Roman past and its earlier history. It was fairly easy to learn about medieval times because many of these objects had survived, including religious artefacts, armour, books and buildings. But antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and earlier sites, such as Stonehenge, in a more scientific and systematic way and to learn more about the earliest Britons.  This knowledge, combined with the new study of rock strata, led archaeologists to question the accepted date of the world's creation – 4004 BC – calculated from readings of the Bible.

This new knowledge, combined with the study of rock layers (called strata), led archaeologists to question the widely accepted date of the world's creation. That date was 4004 BC, which had been calculated from readings of the Bible.

CuratorialText_x: In 1824, Charles Townley's famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures, acquired on his Grand Tours in Italy, came to the British Museum. They joined the collections of prints and drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases and other classical antiquities from fellow connoisseurs such as Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These men studied each other's collections to learn about the 'progress' of art, from 'primitive' beginnings in early civilisations to what they considered to be the height of artistic achievement – the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This became the standard against which to measure all art and led, in late 18th-century Britain, to a classical revival in architecture and the decorative arts.

CuratorialText_x: Everything gathered in the 18th century had to be classified and organised so that it could be more easily understood and used.  King George III's library was presented to the nation by George IV and was housed in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (his library is now in the British Library). The coins and medals collection was among the first part of the library to arrive at the Museum and they were arranged by country and ruler to help bring past history alive. George III was also keenly interested in the navy, navigation and scientific discoveries and collected instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton arranged his vases found in the area around Naples according to the styles of painting, attempting to establish a chronology for them. He later published them in beautifully illustrated folios. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious 'artificial' (manmade) objects from around the world, including those collected from enslaved peoples in Jamaica. These objects were catalogued and displayed in many different ways – by type, material, date or purpose.
