CuratorialText_x: The Enlightenment led to a fevered interest in deciphering the mysterious forms of Egyptian hieroglyphs and the cuneiform inscriptions found in lands described in the Bible as well as early forms of Sanskrit in India. Scholars 'cracked the codes' in the 19th century, opening up thousands of years of history. At the same time, antiquaries deciphered runes and other early forms of writing, revealing more of our own history. This was a period when significant numbers of antiquities, acquired from collectors, diplomats and through excavation, entered the British Museum.

Scholars 'cracked the codes' in the 19th century, opening up thousands of years of history. At the same time, antiquaries deciphered runes and other early forms of writing, revealing more of our own history. This was a period when significant numbers of antiquities, acquired from collectors, diplomats and through excavation, entered the British Museum.
persona1_adapted:
During the Enlightenment, people became very curious about ancient writing. They wanted to understand Egyptian hieroglyphs—picture symbols used in ancient Egypt—and cuneiform, a wedge-shaped writing from the Middle East. These writings appeared in lands mentioned in the Bible. Scholars also studied early forms of Sanskrit, an old language from India.

In the 1800s, experts "cracked the codes" of these ancient scripts. This opened up thousands of years of history. At the same time, other researchers decoded runes—letters used by early Germanic peoples—and other old writing systems. This helped reveal more about our own past.

During this time, many ancient objects came into the British Museum. They were collected from private collectors, diplomats, and through digging at ancient sites.

In the 19th century, scholars successfully "cracked the codes" of these ancient scripts, revealing thousands of years of previously unknown history. At the same time, antiquaries—historians who study ancient objects—deciphered runes and other early writing systems, uncovering more about our own past. This era also saw a large number of ancient artifacts, acquired from collectors, diplomats, and through archaeological digs, entering the British Museum.

CuratorialText_x: Christian mistrust of superstition and idols, rituals and magic led to ignorance about other religions. Enlightenment scholars attempted to dispel this by studying the ancient and modern religions they encountered. They researched rituals and cults in different cultures, collected religious artefacts, grouped gods into families and examined their attributes and functions. They wanted to find similarities that might shed light on the origins of the world's religions. Much of this activity took place within the context of colonialism.

CuratorialText_x: In this era of colonialism and global trade, travellers, traders, scientists, explorers and diplomats created collections of ethnographic objects, including ceremonial and everyday items, thought representative of customs and cultures of people around the globe. They collected artefacts and published illustrated accounts about the lives and cultures of people around the world, from a European perspective. Alongside Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also houses objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti and other Pacific islands. A significant number of objects were acquired through employees of the East India and Royal Africa Companies, whose wealth was made possible through the use of enslaved labour and the exploitation of people and resources.

CuratorialText_x: During his leadership, from 336–323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states, the Persian Empire and founded more than 70 cities creating an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles.  The Hellenistic period dates from the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC to the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at Actium in 31 BC. After his death, Alexander's empire was divided up into kingdoms, ruled by his generals. The major dynasties sponsored major architectural programmes and were great patrons of the arts and culture. Objects on display in Room 22 include a colossal carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), the Demeter from Knidos in south-west Turkey and Greek bronzes. Displays of smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters and coroplasts (makers of clay or terracotta figurines) and gem cutters.

Alexander the Great led his empire from 336 to 323 BC. During that time, he conquered the Greek city-states and the Persian Empire. He also founded more than 70 cities. His empire stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles.

The Hellenistic period began after Alexander died in 323 BC. It lasted until 31 BC, when Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony were defeated at Actium. After Alexander's death, his empire was split into kingdoms. His generals ruled these kingdoms. The major royal families paid for large building projects. They also supported art and culture.

Room 22 displays many objects from this time. One is a huge carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos. That temple was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. Another object is the Demeter statue from Knidos in southwest Turkey. There are also Greek bronzes. Smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters, and gem cutters. Coroplasts—makers of clay or terracotta figurines—also created detailed works.

The major ruling families of these kingdoms sponsored large-scale building projects and were generous supporters of the arts and culture. CuratorialText_x: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.

CuratorialText_x: This fashion show presents contemporary clothing of the Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa (the great ocean of Kiwa/the Pacific Ocean), which has been facilitated in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia. The collection has been conceived and made by Māori designer Lyall Hakaraia in collaboration with venerated practitioners from across the Pacific. These practictioners are tohunga (experts) who are building and continue to grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They consider themselves as guides and strive to be 'good ancestors' through their daily practices, powerful intentions and vision. They are building resilient futures for mokopuna (grandchildren) and generations to come. The show will act as a focal point for the Pacific diaspora community as they activate a VĀ (portal) to their ancestors. The show is an offering to those ancestors that remain at the British Museum and are embodied through Pacific collections. It aims to 'warm' them in slumber. The collective will sing, chant and offer prayers to them during the show with Aroha (love) from across the Pacific.

The displays feature the richness of art and material culture in China, including painting, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer and ceramics.

CuratorialText_x: The other half of the gallery presents South Asia's many histories chronologically and by region, from early human occupation to the present. Highlights include seals from the Indus civilisation, superb south Indian sculptures of Shiva and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara from Sri Lanka. Sophisticated paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors can be seen alongside 20th-century paintings, including by the Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore.

CuratorialText_x: China is one of the world's oldest civilisations. Today it covers a vast territory the size of Europe and is home to a quarter of the world's population. China has produced a highly distinctive culture with beautifully crafted objects made on an industrial scale from the earliest times. Room 33 examines the past 7,000 years of China's history, exploring the themes of writing systems, rituals, beliefs, war, international trade and more. Room 33 also explores South Asia's history, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day. Trade, exchange, migration and political power are some of the themes examined. South Asia spans India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau and Sri Lanka. The region is home to numerous ancient and modern languages, such as Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi and Urdu. The Buddhist, Hindu, Jain, Sikh and Islamic faiths have inspired magnificent architecture, sculpture, painting, literature and music.

CuratorialText_x:
Africa's rich cultural heritage and histories are represented at the Museum both in Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and across the galleries. Join our volunteer-led tours, developed in collaboration with community members, to explore their personal selection of objects ranging from the ancient world to the present day. Each guide will deliver their own personal tour exploring one of the following themes:
Out of Africa: stories of migration, materials and mythology
The Igbo worldview: artefacts from the past in the British Museum
African metal artefacts: tradition, technique and trade
Ancient objects from African kingdoms

CuratorialText_x: Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704–681 BC and was famous for his building projects. The rooms and courtyards of his Neo-Assyrian Southwest Palace at Nineveh were decorated with a series of detailed carved stone panels. Many of these stone panels are on display in Room 9. The panels depict a variety of scenes, including the transport of huge sculptures of human-headed winged bulls (lamassu) that weigh up to 30 tons and were intended for the main entrances to the palace. These illustrations provide an insight into ancient quarrying and transport techniques, as well as Sennacherib's keen interest in his building projects. Other panels on display depict the king's military campaigns.

Other panels in the room depict the king’s military campaigns.

CuratorialText_x: Split across three rooms, these galleries display life during this period in Assyrian culture. On display are lion hunting reliefs, the siege of the city of Lachish and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad.  Visitors to Room 10a are greeted by a pair of colossal human-headed winged bulls. Intended as a supernatural guardians, these gigantic figures guarded the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad, and were intended to deter evil of all kinds from entering.

CuratorialText_x: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was considered the sport of kings, symbolic of the ruling monarch's duty to protect and fight for his people. The sculpted reliefs in Room 10a illustrate the sporting exploits of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC) and were created for his palace at Nineveh (in modern-day northern Iraq). The hunt scenes, full of tension and realism, rank among the finest achievements of Assyrian Art. They depict the release of the lions, the ensuing chase and subsequent killing.

The hunt scenes, full of tension and realism, rank among the finest achievements of Assyrian Art. They depict the release of the lions, the ensuing chase and subsequent killing.

They are some of the finest examples of Assyrian art. The pictures show the lions being set free, the chase that follows, and the final kill.

CuratorialText_x: Lachish was one of the chief cities of the kingdom of Judah in the southern Levant and in 701 BC it was captured by the Assyrian King Sennacherib (704–681 BC). The siege followed the refusal of Lachish to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire (based in modern northern Iraq) and is mentioned in the Bible. Many of the relief sculptures on display in Room 10b depict the capture of the city, alongside a selection of items and weaponry used in the siege. A 'prism' inscribed with an Assyrian account of the campaign is also on show.
CuratorialText_y: Lachish was one of the chief cities of the kingdom of Judah in the southern Levant and in 701 BC it was captured by the Assyrian King Sennacherib (704–681 BC). The siege followed the refusal of Lachish to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire (based in modern northern Iraq) and is mentioned in the Bible. Many of the relief sculptures on display in Room 10b depict the capture of the city, alongside a selection of items and weaponry used in the siege. A 'prism' inscribed with an Assyrian account of the campaign is also on show.
persona1_adapted:
Lachish was one of the main cities in the kingdom of Judah, located in the southern part of the ancient Near East. In 701 BC, the Assyrian King Sennacherib (who ruled from 704 to 681 BC) captured the city. This happened because Lachish refused to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire, which was based in what is now northern Iraq. The Bible also mentions this siege.

In Room 10b, you can see many stone reliefs (carved pictures) that show the capture of Lachish. There are also objects and weapons used during the siege. A special clay prism with an Assyrian account of the campaign is on display too.

The siege occurred because Lachish refused to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire, which was based in what is now northern Iraq. This event is mentioned in the Bible. Many of the relief sculptures displayed in Room 10b depict the capture of the city. They are shown alongside a selection of items and weaponry used during the siege. persona3_adapted: Lachish was one of the major cities of the kingdom of Judah, located in the southern part of the ancient Near East (roughly modern-day Israel and Palestine). In 701 BC, it was conquered by the Assyrian king Sennacherib (who ruled from 704 to 681 BC). The siege began after Lachish refused to pay tribute—essentially a forced payment or tax—to the Assyrian Empire, which was based in what is now northern Iraq. This event is also mentioned in the Bible. Many of the carved stone panels, or reliefs, on display in Room 10b show scenes from the city's capture, along with a selection of objects and weapons used during the siege. Also on view is a clay prism inscribed with the Assyrian version of the campaign.

CuratorialText_x: The city and palace at Khorsabad (in modern northern Iraq), was built for the Assyrian King Sargon II (721–705 BC). The palace entrances were originally dominated by pairs of colossal human-headed winged bulls, which were intended as guardians, accompanied by protective spirits with magical powers. Two of these impressive statues now stand in Room 10c, along with carvings depicting the king and crown prince, royal courtiers and hunting scenes. Inscriptions on display in the gallery come from a similar winged bull from the palace of Sennacherib (704–681 BC) at nearby Nineveh and were badly burnt when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.

These inscriptions were badly burned when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.

CuratorialText_x: Rooms 57–59 present the material culture of the region from the Neolithic farmers of the eighth millennium BC to the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC, within the context of major historical events. Objects on display illustrate the continuity of the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant throughout this period. They highlight the indigenous origins of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. The display compares this culture with that of the people of central inland Syria, the Amorites and the Aramaeans.
CuratorialText_y: Rooms 57–59 present the material culture of the region from the Neolithic farmers of the eighth millennium BC to the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC, within the context of major historical events. Objects on display illustrate the continuity of the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant throughout this period. They highlight the indigenous origins of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. The display compares this culture with that of the people of central inland Syria, the Amorites and the Aramaeans.
persona1_adapted:
Rooms 57–59 show objects from a region that was home to many ancient peoples. These objects date from around 8000 BC, when the first farmers lived there, to 539 BC, when the Neo-Babylonian Empire fell. The items on display help tell the story of major historical events.

The objects show how the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant (an area that includes modern-day Israel, Palestine, and Jordan) lasted for thousands of years. They also highlight that both the Israelites and the Phoenicians had local roots in this region. The display compares the Canaanite culture with that of people from central inland Syria, including the Amorites and the Aramaeans.
persona2_adapted: Rooms 57–59 present the material culture of the region, from the Neolithic farmers of the eighth millennium BC to the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC. This is shown within the context of major historical events. The objects on display illustrate the continuity of Canaanite culture in the southern Levant throughout this period. They highlight the indigenous origins of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. The display also compares this culture with that of the people of central inland Syria, specifically the Amorites and the Aramaeans.
persona3_adapted: Rooms 57–59 showcase the everyday and ceremonial objects made and used by people in this region, from the early farming communities of the eighth millennium BC up to the collapse of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC. The items on display are arranged to show how the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant—an area roughly covering modern-day Israel, Palestine, Jordan, and Lebanon—remained remarkably consistent over thousands of years. These artifacts also reveal that both the Israelites and the Phoenicians had their roots in this local Canaanite tradition. The exhibition sets this culture alongside that of the Amorites and Aramaeans, peoples who lived in the inland areas of central Syria, allowing visitors to compare their ways of life.

CuratorialText_x: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu form an important land link between Europe and Asia. They lie where the modern Republic of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia and north-west Iran are located today.  Examples of Early Bronze Age craftsmanship on display include silver bull and cup. Business archives of Middle Bronze Age merchants illustrate trading between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). Delicate gold jewellery and figurines date from the Hittite period. Iron Age objects from Urartu, include winged bulls and griffins used to decorate furniture.

Business archives of Middle Bronze Age merchants illustrate trading between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). Delicate gold jewellery and figurines date from the Hittite period. Iron Age objects from Urartu, include winged bulls and griffins used to decorate furniture.

These regions are located in what is now the Republic of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia, and northwestern Iran. Among the Early Bronze Age works on display are a silver bull and a cup. Commercial archives from the Middle Bronze Age document trade between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (present-day Iraq). persona3_adapted: Ancient Anatolia and the kingdom of Urartu served as a vital land bridge connecting Europe and Asia, in what is now modern-day Turkey, Armenia, Georgia, and northwestern Iran. Among the objects on display from the Early Bronze Age are a silver bull and a cup, showcasing the skilled metalwork of that era. Records from Middle Bronze Age merchants—essentially ancient business archives—reveal the trade networks that linked central Anatolia with Mesopotamia, present-day Iraq. From the Hittite period come delicate gold jewelry and small human or animal figures. Objects from the Iron Age kingdom of Urartu include winged bulls and griffins, mythical creatures that were used as decorative elements on furniture.

CuratorialText_x: Reaching back more than 2,000 years, this exhibition explored the origins of Hindu, Jain and Buddhist sacred art in the ancient and powerful nature spirits of India, and the spread of this art beyond the subcontinent.  One of the first major exhibitions in the world to look at the early devotional art of India from a multi-faith, contemporary and global perspective, it highlighted the inspiration behind now-familiar depictions of the deities and enlightened teachers of these world religions – and how they were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.

It was one of the first major exhibitions in the world to study early devotional art from India. It did so from a multi-faith, modern, and global viewpoint. The exhibition highlighted the inspiration behind now-familiar images of gods and enlightened teachers from these world religions. It also showed how these images were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.

CuratorialText_x: Colourful, multi-sensory and atmospheric, this exhibition was developed in collaboration with an advisory community panel of practising Hindus, Buddhists and Jains. These living religious traditions and their sacred art are now integral to the daily lives of almost two billion people around the world including in the UK. Key loans from our community partners helped to tell this contemporary story.

CuratorialText_x: The exhibition showcased more than 180 objects – including sculptures, paintings, drawings and manuscripts – from the South Asian collection at the British Museum as well as generous loans from national and international partners. It highlighted provenance, examining the stories, from creation to acquisition by museums, of every object in the show.

CuratorialText_x: From the symbolic footprints which preceded portrayals of the Buddha in human form to the cosmic serpents incorporated into Hindu art and the nature spirits who attend Jain enlightened teachers, this compelling exhibition told the ancient stories behind these living traditions.
CuratorialText_y: From the symbolic footprints which preceded portrayals of the Buddha in human form to the cosmic serpents incorporated into Hindu art and the nature spirits who attend Jain enlightened teachers, this compelling exhibition told the ancient stories behind these living traditions.

It also includes cosmic serpents in Hindu art. Nature spirits attend Jain enlightened teachers. These artworks bring these old stories to life.
persona2_adapted: From the symbolic footprints that appeared before images of the Buddha in human form, to the cosmic serpents included in Hindu art, and the nature spirits that accompany Jain enlightened teachers, this compelling exhibition presented the ancient stories behind these living traditions.
persona3_adapted: From the symbolic footprints that once stood in for images of the Buddha—before artists began showing him in human form—to the cosmic serpents woven into Hindu art, and the nature spirits that accompany Jain enlightened teachers, this compelling exhibition revealed the ancient stories behind these still-living traditions.

CuratorialText_x: The first exhibition on Hiroshige to be held at the British Museum, and the first on the artist in London for more than a quarter of a century, this was a visually stunning portrait of a country about to change forever. Born during an unsettled time in Japan's history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) went on to become one of the country's most talented, prolific and popular artists. As Japan confronted the encroaching outside world, Hiroshige's calm artistic vision connected with – and reassured – people at every level of society. From fashionable figures and energetic city views to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world, Hiroshige captured many aspects of life in the Japan of his time. Stunning bird-and-flower prints reveal his poetic feeling for nature while his evocative landscapes reflected the growing interest in travel across Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often the way he imagined it could be.

From fashionable figures and energetic city views to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world, Hiroshige captured many aspects of life in the Japan of his time. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often the way he imagined it could be.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition is the first on Hiroshige at the British Museum. It is also the first show on the artist in London in over 25 years. The display offers a stunning look at a country about to change forever.

Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) was born during an unsettled time in Japan's history. He became one of the country's most talented, productive, and popular artists. As Japan faced the outside world moving in, Hiroshige's calm art connected with people at every level of society. It gave them comfort.

Hiroshige captured many sides of life in his Japan. He showed fashionable people and busy city views. He also painted remote landscapes and scenes from nature. His beautiful bird-and-flower prints reveal his poetic feeling for nature. His landscapes reflect the growing interest in travel across Japan. Hiroshige sometimes showed his world as it really was. But often, he showed it the way he imagined it could be.

Born during a period of instability in Japan's history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) became one of the nation's most gifted, prolific, and beloved artists. As Japan faced increasing pressure from the outside world, Hiroshige's serene artistic vision connected with—and offered reassurance to—people across all levels of society. His work captured many facets of life in Japan at the time: from fashionable figures and dynamic city views to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world. Stunning bird-and-flower prints reveal his poetic sensitivity to nature, while his evocative landscapes reflect a growing interest in travel throughout Japan. Hiroshige depicted his world sometimes as it was, but often as he imagined it could be.
persona3_adapted: The first exhibition on Hiroshige at the British Museum—and the first in London in over twenty-five years—offered a visually striking portrait of a country on the verge of profound change. Born during a turbulent period in Japan’s history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) became one of the nation’s most gifted, productive, and beloved artists. As Japan faced increasing pressure from the outside world, Hiroshige’s calm artistic vision connected with—and offered reassurance to—people across all levels of society. From fashionable figures and lively city scenes to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world, he captured many aspects of life in the Japan of his time. Stunning prints of birds and flowers reveal his poetic sensitivity to nature, while his evocative landscapes reflect a growing fascination with travel across Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often as he imagined it could be.

CuratorialText_x: Possessed of remarkable technical skills, both as a colourist and draftsman, Hiroshige had a sympathetic regard for people from all walks of life. Unlike most other print designers of his day, he came from a samurai family, but crossed social boundaries to devote himself to depicting popular customs. His work was affordable, too – along with celebrated landscape prints, he also designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to all.
CuratorialText_y: Possessed of remarkable technical skills, both as a colourist and draftsman, Hiroshige had a sympathetic regard for people from all walks of life. Unlike most other print designers of his day, he came from a samurai family, but crossed social boundaries to devote himself to depicting popular customs. His work was affordable, too – along with celebrated landscape prints, he also designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to all.
persona1_adapted: Hiroshige had amazing technical skills as a colorist and draftsman. He felt sympathy for people from all walks of life. Unlike most other print designers of his time, he came from a samurai family. But he crossed social boundaries to focus on showing popular customs. His work was also affordable. Besides his famous landscape prints, he designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that anyone could buy.
persona2_adapted: Possessing remarkable technical skills as both a colourist and a draftsman, Hiroshige showed a sympathetic regard for people from all social classes. Unlike most other print designers of his time, he came from a samurai family. However, he crossed social boundaries to dedicate himself to depicting popular customs. His work was also affordable. In addition to his celebrated landscape prints, he designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to everyone.
persona3_adapted: A master of both color and line, Hiroshige possessed remarkable technical skill and a genuine warmth toward people from every level of society. Unlike most other print designers of his time—who came from common backgrounds—he was born into a samurai family, yet he crossed class boundaries to focus on depicting everyday customs and popular life. His work was also affordable: in addition to his famous landscape prints, he designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that almost anyone could buy.

CuratorialText_x: The exhibition featured prints, drawings, illustrated books and paintings from the British Museum collection, as well as a significant gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a major US collector of Hiroshige's work, and other important loans. As well as exploring Hiroshige's incredible body of work, this show considered his global legacy, which spans from Japan's Edo period (1615–1868) through to Vincent van Gogh and contemporary artists such as Julian Opie.

The exhibition explores Hiroshige's amazing body of work. It also looks at his global legacy. That legacy stretches from Japan's Edo period (1615–1868) to artists like Vincent van Gogh and modern artists such as Julian Opie.
persona2_adapted: The exhibition included prints, drawings, illustrated books, and paintings from the British Museum collection. It also featured a major gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, an important US collector of Hiroshige’s work, along with other significant loans. In addition to examining Hiroshige’s remarkable oeuvre, the exhibition addressed his global legacy. This legacy extends from Japan’s Edo period (1615–1868) to Vincent van Gogh and contemporary artists such as Julian Opie.

Beyond showcasing Hiroshige’s remarkable range of work, the exhibition examines his lasting influence around the world—from Japan’s Edo period (1615–1868) to artists like Vincent van Gogh and contemporary figures such as Julian Opie.

CuratorialText_x: Pair of bride's slippers (tarkasin) made of red-dyed leather embroidered in silk thread and decorated with metal studs. Tongues cut in the shape of the khamsa ('five') or 'Hand of Fatima'.
CuratorialText_y: Pair of bride's slippers (tarkasin) made of red-dyed leather embroidered in silk thread and decorated with metal studs. Tongues cut in the shape of the khamsa ('five') or 'Hand of Fatima'.
persona1_adapted: These are a pair of bride's slippers, called *tarkasin*. They are made from red-dyed leather. The slippers are embroidered with silk thread and decorated with metal studs. The tongue of each slipper is cut in the shape of the *khamsa*, which means "five" in Arabic. This shape is also known as the "Hand of Fatima."
persona2_adapted: Pair of bride's slippers (tarkasin). They are made of red-dyed leather, embroidered with silk thread, and decorated with metal studs. The tongues are cut in the shape of the khamsa ('five'), also known as the 'Hand of Fatima.'

CuratorialText_x: Head and upper body of pink/grey granite monumental statue of Ramses II (one of a pair placed before the door of the Ramesseum) wearing nemes head-cloth and circlet of uraei (about half now lost), the sculptor has exploited the bichrome nature of the stone to emphasise the division between body and face; the dorsal pillar is inscribed with vertical registers of hieroglyphs - giving the name and titles of the king and part of a dedication to Amun-Ra; in 1817 it was noted that there were traces of colour upon the statue and it may have, therefore, been painted red in antiquity.

persona1_adapted:
This statue shows the head and upper body of Ramses II. It is made of pink and gray granite. It was one of a pair of statues placed in front of the door of the Ramesseum, a temple built for the king.

The king wears a royal headdress called a *nemes* head-cloth. He also wears a circlet of uraei—a band of cobras, a symbol of royal power. About half of that circlet is now missing.

The sculptor used the two colors of the stone on purpose. The pink and gray help separate the king’s face from his body. The back pillar of the statue has rows of hieroglyphs carved into it. These symbols give the king’s name and titles. They also include part of a dedication to the god Amun-Ra.

In 1817, people noticed traces of color on the statue. It may have been painted red in ancient times.
persona2_adapted: Head and upper body of a pink/grey granite monumental statue of Ramses II (one of a pair originally placed before the door of the Ramesseum). The statue wears a nemes head-cloth and a circlet of uraei (about half of this is now lost). The sculptor used the bichrome nature of the stone to emphasize the division between body and face. The dorsal pillar is inscribed with vertical registers of hieroglyphs. These give the name and titles of the king, as well as part of a dedication to Amun-Ra. In 1817, it was noted that there were traces of color on the statue. Therefore, it may have been painted red in antiquity.
persona3_adapted: Head and upper body of a monumental statue of Ramses II, carved from pink and grey granite. This statue was originally one of a pair placed before the entrance of the Ramesseum, the king's memorial temple. The figure wears the *nemes*—the striped headcloth often associated with Egyptian royalty—and a circlet of *uraei* (sacred cobras), though about half of this crown is now missing. The sculptor made clever use of the stone's two-tone coloring to create a clear visual separation between the body and the face. On the pillar running down the statue's back, vertical columns of hieroglyphs record the king's name and titles, along with part of a dedication to the god Amun-Ra. In 1817, observers noted traces of color on the statue, suggesting it may originally have been painted red in ancient times.

CuratorialText_x: Brass (heavily leaded zinc-brass) head cast using the lost wax (cire perdue) technique. The head is a little under life size and is made in a naturalistic style. It has a headdress, suggesting a crown, of complex construction. The main tubular section of the crown runs around the head in a three-layer composition. The upper layer has a band of four horizontal rectangles representing flat, discoidal beads surmounted by a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel. The central layer has a row of vertical rectangles representing tubular beads with tassels. The lower layer has a row of rosettes painted in red. The main tubular section of the crown also has a projecting arc around the forehead comprising small tubular beads edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. The central part of the cover has eighteen vertical elements incised to indicate plaitwork with traces of black paint. The bottom and sides have a row of red-painted rosettes. A crest rises above the central crown at the front. It comprises a conical roundel with central boss surrounded by seven concentric rings, probably representing beads. A plaitwork element rises behind the roundel, it terminates in a pointed ovoid tip; both elements bear traces of black paint. The face is slightly elongated and has vertical incised markings (striations). Two lines of holes run between the lobes of the ears, one going through the angle of the neck and the jaw, the second crossing the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip. The almond-shaped eyes are small in proportion to the head and the eyebrow-ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not striated. There are grooves round the front of the neck representing skin creases. There are holes pierced through the neck at the front and at both sides. There is a large irregular hole on the right proper side of the jaw.

persona2_adapted: Egyptian terracotta model palmette acroterion. The upper corner is broken off from a miniature altar. Each side of the altar is decorated with a half-palmette. persona3_adapted: This small decorative object is an Egyptian terracotta ornament shaped like a stylized fan of leaves, known as a palmette. It was originally attached to the upper corner of a miniature altar, which has since broken off. Both visible sides of the ornament are decorated with a half-palmette design. The altar itself was made using a multi-part mold, likely consisting of two pieces, though possibly more. Traces of a matte red coating, called a slip, survive on both the interior and exterior surfaces. There are also signs of burning inside the altar. The object is made from brown Nile silt clay, which has a light-brown core and contains abundant fine gold mica as well as some white inclusions.

CuratorialText_x: Indigo resist-dyed cloth (adire eleko); on factory-made cotton; two panels machine sewn tgether. Each panel is divided into four by seven squares. Hand-painted patterns include two squares with the pillars of Mapo Hall (in Ibadan) between which are four pestles (or spoons).
CuratorialText_y: Indigo resist-dyed cloth (adire eleko); on factory-made cotton; two panels machine sewn tgether. Each panel is divided into four by seven squares. Hand-painted patterns include two squares with the pillars of Mapo Hall (in Ibadan) between which are four pestles (or spoons).
persona1_adapted:
This cloth is called *adire eleko*. It is a type of indigo-dyed fabric. The dye was applied using a resist technique, where parts of the cloth are protected from the dye to create patterns.

The cloth is made from factory-made cotton. Two panels of this cotton were sewn together by machine. Each panel is divided into a grid of four squares by seven squares.

The patterns were painted by hand. Two of the squares show the pillars of Mapo Hall, a famous building in Ibadan, Nigeria. Between these pillars are four shapes that look like pestles (tools used for grinding) or spoons.
persona2_adapted: Indigo resist-dyed cloth (adire eleko); on factory-made cotton; two panels machine-sewn together. Each panel is divided into four by seven squares. Hand-painted patterns include two squares with the pillars of Mapo Hall (in Ibadan). Between these pillars are four pestles (or spoons).
persona3_adapted: This cloth is a type of indigo-dyed fabric known as *adire eleko*, made using a resist-dyeing technique. It is sewn together from two machine-made cotton panels. Each panel is divided into a grid of four rows and seven columns. The patterns were painted by hand and include two squares featuring the pillars of Mapo Hall, a historic building in Ibadan, Nigeria. Between these pillars are four shapes that resemble pestles or spoons.

CuratorialText_x: Pottery Protocorinthian alabastron decorated with a lion and two griffins; wheel-made; depressed-ovoid body on a rounded base; small mouth with a sloping rim; pierced lug handle at the top; made of fine buff clay and slip, with painted decoration which has fired black; also added purple paint on the animal bodies; details of the figures are incised. The main frieze shows two griffins separated by stylised tree-like motif; a lion occupies the space between the two mythical beasts; filling motifs in the field. Above the main scene is a frieze of stylised floral designs; below simpler motifs composed of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. Simple rays on the vase, and tongues on the lip.
CuratorialText_y: Pottery Protocorinthian alabastron decorated with a lion and two griffins; wheel-made; depressed-ovoid body on a rounded base; small mouth with a sloping rim; pierced lug handle at the top; made of fine buff clay and slip, with painted decoration which has fired black; also added purple paint on the animal bodies; details of the figures are incised. The main frieze shows two griffins separated by stylised tree-like motif; a lion occupies the space between the two mythical beasts; filling motifs in the field. Above the main scene is a frieze of stylised floral designs; below simpler motifs composed of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. Simple rays on the vase, and tongues on the lip.
persona1_adapted:
This is a small pottery container called an alabastron. It was made in ancient Corinth. The potter shaped it on a wheel. The body is round and sits on a curved base. The mouth is small with a slanted rim. A small handle sticks out from the top.

The artist used fine, light-colored clay and a thin coating called slip. The painted decoration turned black when fired. The artist also added purple paint on the animals. Details of the animals were scratched into the clay.

The main band of decoration shows two griffins. A griffin is a mythical creature with the head and wings of an eagle and the body of a lion. A tree-like design separates the two griffins. A lion fills the space between them. Small shapes fill the empty areas around the animals.

Above the main scene is a band of flower-like designs. Below are simpler patterns made of dots and S-shaped lines. The bottom of the vase has simple lines like rays. The lip has tongue-shaped designs.
persona2_adapted:
Pottery Protocorinthian alabastron decorated with a lion and two griffins. It is wheel-made. The body is depressed-ovoid on a rounded base. The mouth is small with a sloping rim. At the top, there is a pierced lug handle. The vase is made of fine buff clay and slip. The painted decoration has fired black. Purple paint was added on the animal bodies. Details of the figures are incised.

The main frieze shows two griffins separated by a stylized tree-like motif. A lion occupies the space between the two mythical beasts. There are filling motifs in the field. Above the main scene is a frieze of stylized floral designs. Below are simpler motifs composed of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. There are simple rays on the vase and tongues on the lip.
persona3_adapted:
This small pottery container, known as an alabastron, was made in ancient Corinth using a potter's wheel. It has a rounded, egg-shaped body, a narrow mouth with a sloping rim, and a small pierced handle at the top. The vessel is made from fine, light-colored clay, coated with a thin liquid clay called slip, and then painted with a black-fired decoration. Additional purple paint was applied to the bodies of the animals, and the details of the figures were carved into the surface.

The main band of decoration shows two griffins—mythical creatures with the body of a lion and the head of an eagle—separated by a stylized, tree-like design. A lion fills the space between these two mythical beasts, and smaller decorative motifs fill the background. Above this main scene is a band of stylized floral patterns, while below are simpler designs made of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. The vase also features simple ray-like lines, and the lip is decorated with tongue-shaped patterns.

CuratorialText_x: IWM’s Sound Collection is home to over 35,000 recordings relating to conflicts since 1914.  It is the largest oral history archive of its kind in the world, with more than 60,000 hours of material and interviews in over 15 different languages.  Beyond interviews it also includes speeches, sound effects, radio broadcasts and music. The collection covers the wartime experiences of ordinary men and women- from soldiers and activists, to factory workers and journalists. It also includes notable figures such as Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and Vera Lynn.  Our unique collection of oral history interviews has formed the basis for significant academic research as well as featuring heavily in bestselling books, films, TV and radio broadcasts and podcasts. Many thousands of our audio recordings can be listened to via the IWM website, which is regularly updated with new additions.

CuratorialText_x:
IWM has managed a Film Archive for over a century. From its very beginnings as an institution IWM understood the importance of film as primary source material and its extensive collection illustrates the causes, course and consequences of conflict from the First World War to the present day. A remarkable resource for researchers and film-makers
Our constantly growing collection now extends to over 25,000 hours of moving images, representing a wide and diverse range of material from public and service information films, documentaries and unedited combat film, through to official newsreels and amateur films. Two substantial collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia are also held. An ongoing film digitisation programme provides free online access, with newly-digitised media added as soon as it becomes available.
CuratorialText_y:

A remarkable resource for researchers and film-makers
Our constantly growing collection now extends to over 25,000 hours of moving images, representing a wide and diverse range of material from public and service information films, documentaries and unedited combat film, through to official newsreels and amateur films. Two substantial collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia are also held. An ongoing film digitisation programme provides free online access, with newly-digitised media added as soon as it becomes available.

This large collection shows the causes, course, and consequences of conflict from World War I to today. It is a valuable resource for researchers and filmmakers.

Our collection keeps growing. It now has over 25,000 hours of moving images. This includes a wide range of material: public and service information films, documentaries, unedited combat footage, official newsreels, and amateur films. We also hold two large collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia. A film digitisation programme—converting old film into digital files—gives free online access. Newly-digitised media is added as soon as it becomes available.

Our constantly growing collection now includes more than 25,000 hours of moving images. It covers a wide and diverse range of material: public and service information films, documentaries, unedited combat footage, official newsreels, and amateur films. We also hold two substantial collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia. An ongoing film digitization program provides free online access, with newly digitized media added as soon as it becomes available.

She was Worsley's first ship and he remained with her until she was sunk off Foreland, Ulster by the U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940. persona1_adapted:
**The Sinking of HMS Laurentic**

This painting shows the moment HMS Laurentic was sinking. Half of the ship is underwater, and its front end (the bow) sticks up into the air. In the front of the painting, a lifeboat filled with sailors glows in the dark, lit by a small light. Another lifeboat appears on the right, and tiny dots of light in the background show more lifeboats. On the left, you can see the top of the German submarine (called a U-boat) that attacked the ship.

HMS Laurentic started as a passenger ship for the P&O shipping company. In 1939, it was fitted with 6-inch guns and turned into an armed merchant cruiser—a warship that also carried cargo. This was the first ship the artist, Frank Worsley, served on. He stayed with it until it was sunk off the coast of Foreland, Ulster, by German U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer on the night of November 3, 1940.

Worsley painted the first version of this scene right after the attack, while on leave as a survivor. The War Artists' Advisory Committee (WAAC) bought that painting. In August 1942, they sent it to South America as part of a traveling show of war art. But on the way, the ship carrying it was torpedoed and the painting was lost. So, at the WAAC's request, Worsley painted this replacement.

HMS Laurentic was originally a P&O liner. In 1939, it was equipped with 6-inch guns and converted into an armed merchant cruiser. This was Worsley’s first ship. He remained with her until she was sunk off Foreland, Ulster, by the U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940.

Worsley painted the original version immediately after the event, while on survivors’ leave. The WAAC (War Artists' Advisory Committee) bought the painting. In August 1942, they sent it to South America as part of a touring exhibition of war art. However, during transport, the ship carrying the painting was torpedoed. As a result, the present picture was painted as a replacement, at the request of the WAAC. (Source: exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)
persona3_adapted:
On the night of November 3, 1940, the British ship HMS Laurentic was sunk off the coast of Ulster by a German U-boat commanded by Otto Kretschmer. This painting shows the moment of the sinking: in the background, half of the ship is already underwater, with its bow rising into the air. In the foreground, a lifeboat filled with sailors is lit by a small light against the darkness. Another lifeboat appears on the right, and tiny points of light in the distance suggest more lifeboats. On the left, the top of the German submarine is visible.

HMS Laurentic began its life as a passenger liner for the P&O shipping company. In 1939, it was fitted with 6-inch guns and turned into an armed merchant cruiser—a civilian ship pressed into military service. This was the first ship the artist, Worsley, served on, and he stayed with it until it was sunk.

Worsley painted the original version of this scene immediately after the event, while on leave as a survivor. The War Artists Advisory Committee (WAAC) bought the painting and, in August 1942, sent it to South America as part of a traveling exhibition of wartime art. But the ship carrying it was torpedoed, and the painting was lost. At the WAAC’s request, Worsley created this replacement version. (Source: exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)

NameOfTheObject: Mildmay Grove looking to the South West
CuratorialText_x: image: A view across a tree-lined road to a high wall bordering a railway cutting. The wall has grafitti on it which reads 'Nuclear state = Police state' followed by an anarchy symbol and then 'One World'. Behind the wall to the right is a large industrial building. Further to the right is a junction with a road leading off to the left.'In the summer of 1983 ... I discovered the old train station in Mildmay Grove and started what became a set of ten drawings of the section of line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. It was while doing the second drawing that I realized the line I was looking at was used several times a week to take nuclear waste from Sizewell [nuclear power station] to Sellafield [nuclear fuel reprocessing plant]. Over the next six weeks, I found it impossible to escape that realization, and it inextricably meshed with every mark I made.' [Eric Rimmington, from the exhibition catalogue 'A World's Waste: Cumbria, Sellafield and Nuclear Reprocessing, A Brewery Arts Centre Touring Exhibition' 1987.] The artist made this series of drawings at a time of intense anti-nuclear protests against both nuclear weapons and the nuclear power industry. The 1979 decision by the British Government to allow American Cruise Missiles to be sited in the UK sparked a series of marches and protests. These included the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, started in 1981 and which was at its height in 1983 when the missiles finally arrived at the Berkshire base. The accident at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA, also in 1979, heightened fears about the safety of nuclear power. Nuclear waste and its transport and storage continues to be a focus of protest and anxiety. The Greater London Assembly commissioned a report into the safety of nuclear waste trains in 2001 for this reason.

persona1_adapted:
**The Artwork and Its Story**

This drawing shows a view down a tree-lined road. On one side, there is a high wall next to a railway cutting. The wall has graffiti that reads "Nuclear state = Police state." It also has an anarchy symbol and the words "One World." Behind the wall on the right is a large industrial building. Further right, there is a road junction with a road leading off to the left.

**The Artist's Experience**

In the summer of 1983, the artist Eric Rimmington discovered an old train station in Mildmay Grove. He started making ten drawings of the railway line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. While working on the second drawing, he realized something important. The train line he was drawing was used several times a week to carry nuclear waste. The waste came from Sizewell nuclear power station and went to Sellafield, a nuclear fuel reprocessing plant.

Over the next six weeks, Rimmington could not stop thinking about this fact. He said it became mixed up with every mark he made in his drawings.

**The Historical Context**

Rimmington made these drawings during a time of strong anti-nuclear protests. People were protesting against both nuclear weapons and nuclear power plants.

In 1979, the British Government decided to allow American Cruise Missiles to be placed in the UK. This decision sparked many marches and protests. One famous protest was the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, which started in 1981. It was at its peak in 1983, when the missiles finally arrived at the base in Berkshire.

Also in 1979, a serious accident happened at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA. This accident made people even more worried about the safety of nuclear power.

**The Issue Today**

Nuclear waste, and how it is transported and stored, is still a major concern. People continue to protest about it. In fact, in 2001, the Greater London Assembly ordered a report on the safety of nuclear waste trains. This shows that the issue remains important even today.

NameOfTheObject: Evacuees in a Cottage at Cookham Children in Wartime - Five lithographs by Ethel Gabain

persona1_adapted:
**Object Description**  
Five kids sit around a table in a room lit by a big window. An older woman sits in the middle with the youngest child on her lap. A large pudding called "Spotted-Dick" sits on the table, with bowls and forks around it. The woman serves a boy on the far right, who holds out his bowl for a portion.

**Label**  
This is Mrs. Norris from Cookham. She is taking care of five children from London. These kids are part of a "war family" of seven children. By January 1941, they had been living with her for sixteen months.  

Mrs. Norris is an ideal foster parent. Many other homeowners like her also took in children during the war. Over a million kids were moved from dangerous areas in school groups. With so many children, finding homes for them mainly meant placing them with local families.  

Thanks to Mrs. Norris's kindness and care, city kids quickly got used to their new surroundings.
persona2_adapted:
**Object Description**
Five children are seated around a table in a room lit by a large window. An elderly woman sits at the center with the youngest child on her lap. A large "Spotted-Dick" pudding is placed on the table, with several bowls and cutlery arranged around it. The woman serves a boy on the far right, who holds out a bowl for his portion.

**Label**
Mrs. Norris, of Cookham, is shown here with five of her "war family"—seven children from London who, by January 1941, had been living with her for sixteen months. Mrs. Norris is the ideal foster parent, and there are many other householders like her. Over one million children have been moved in school groups from the evacuation areas. With such large numbers, placing children in private homes (called "billets") must always be the main method of finding homes for them. Because of Mrs. Norris's friendliness and care, town children quickly adapt to their new surroundings. (Title from folder cover: *Children in Wartime*.)

**Notes for the reader:**
- *Spotted-Dick*: A traditional British steamed pudding with dried fruit (currants or raisins). The name is idiomatic; "spotted" refers to the fruit, and "dick" is an old dialect word for pudding.
- *Billets*: A military term adopted for wartime housing; here it means private homes where evacuated children were lodged.
- *Evacuation areas*: Urban zones (especially London) considered at risk from bombing during World War II. Children were sent to safer rural regions.
- *War family*: A term used during the war for children temporarily placed with foster families due to evacuation.

**Label**

This photograph shows Mrs. Norris of Cookham, seated at the center with the youngest of her seven foster children on her lap. The children, who were evacuated from London as part of the wartime relocation program, gather around a table in a sunlit room. On the table sits a large "Spotted-Dick" pudding—a traditional British steamed dessert—along with several bowls and utensils. Mrs. Norris is serving a boy on the far right, who holds out his bowl for his portion.

The original caption, taken from the folder cover titled *Children in Wartime*, explains that by January 1941, these children had been living with Mrs. Norris for sixteen months. It describes her as the ideal foster parent, one of many householders who took in evacuated children. Over a million children had been moved from evacuation areas in school-organized groups, and with such large numbers, placing them in private homes—known as billeting—was the main way to find them shelter. The caption notes that, given Mrs. Norris's friendliness and care, city children readily adapted to their new surroundings.

Knight loved painting circus performers and dancers. In this portrait, she focuses on Loftus’s balance and posture while she works.

persona3_adapted:
In this portrait, we see a young woman named Miss Loftus operating a lathe—a machine used for shaping metal. She had been brought to the attention of the War Artist’s Advisory Committee as an exceptionally skilled factory worker. The artist, Laura Knight, initially planned to paint a formal studio portrait, but the Ministry of Supply requested that Loftus be shown at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport. 

At the time, making a Bofors breech ring—a component for an anti-aircraft gun—was considered the most highly skilled job in the factory, normally requiring eight or nine years of training. Loftus was just 21 years old and had no previous factory experience. Her ability to operate the machine was a major public relations success for the war effort. However, some have suggested that she may have been placed at the machine specifically for this reason—to create a powerful image of women’s capabilities in wartime industry.

Industrial machinery was a completely new subject for Knight, who was better known for painting circus performers and dancers. Yet contemporary reports praised the technical accuracy of her work. In taking on this challenge, Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a field traditionally dominated by men. Knight’s interest in the physical grace of performers is evident here: she emphasizes the balance and posture of her subject as she works, turning a factory scene into a study of skill and poise.

CuratorialText_x:
image: The Royal Navy destroyer HMS Eclipse in the process of sinking in the Aegean Sea. The ship appears to have broken in two, with only the stern and bow sections above water. The bow section is on fire, with a bright cloud of flames and smoke billowing up into the night sky. There is another ship in the background and two Greek islands are visible to the left and right.
Label
On 23 October 1943 the two destroyers HMS Eclipse and HMS Petard sailed along the Turkish coast bound for Leros, one of the Greek Dodecanese Islands, which was occupied by a British garrison. On board the Eclipse was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations, Middle East, who was enroute to inspect the defences of Leros. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition and supplies for the garrison. At 11.45pm the Eclipse struck a mine between Kos and Kalymnos, blew up and sank in only two minutes. In addition to the ship's complement of around 200 men, she was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company of the Buffs. Only around 100 men were picked up by an air-sea rescue launch of the RAF dispatched from Leros and boats sent from HMS Petard, after spending over three hours in the water. Davy was amongst the survivors. He went onto become the Commander of Land Forces, Adriatic, retiring from the Army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it had occurred.

persona1_adapted:
On October 23, 1943, two British destroyers—HMS *Eclipse* and HMS *Petard*—sailed near the coast of Turkey. They were heading to Leros, a Greek island held by British troops. On board the *Eclipse* was Brigadier-General George Davy, a high-ranking officer on his way to inspect the island’s defenses. The ships also carried soldiers, ammunition, and supplies.

At 11:45 p.m., the *Eclipse* hit a mine (an underwater explosive) between the islands of Kos and Kalymnos. The ship blew up and sank in just two minutes. Besides its crew of about 200 men, the *Eclipse* was carrying another 200 soldiers. Only around 100 people survived. They were rescued by an RAF air-sea rescue boat from Leros and by boats from HMS *Petard*, after spending more than three hours in the water.

General Davy was among the survivors. He later became the Commander of Land Forces in the Adriatic region and retired from the army in 1949. He wrote about this traumatic event 24 years after it happened.
persona2_adapted:
On 23 October 1943, two destroyers, HMS Eclipse and HMS Petard, sailed along the Turkish coast. Their destination was Leros, one of the Greek Dodecanese Islands. At that time, Leros was occupied by a British garrison. On board the Eclipse was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations, Middle East. He was traveling to inspect the defenses of Leros. The ships also carried troops, ammunition, and supplies for the garrison.

At 11:45 PM, the Eclipse struck a mine between Kos and Kalymnos. The ship exploded and sank in only two minutes. In addition to its own crew of about 200 men, the Eclipse was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company of the Buffs. Only about 100 men were rescued. They were picked up by an air-sea rescue launch from the RAF, sent from Leros, and by boats from HMS Petard. The survivors had spent over three hours in the water.

Davy was among the survivors. He later became the Commander of Land Forces, Adriatic, and retired from the Army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it happened.
persona3_adapted:
On the night of October 23, 1943, two British destroyers—the HMS *Eclipse* and HMS *Petard*—were sailing along the Turkish coast toward the Greek island of Leros, which was then held by a British garrison. Aboard the *Eclipse* was Brigadier-General George Davy, a high-ranking military official traveling to inspect the island’s defenses. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition, and supplies.

At 11:45 p.m., the *Eclipse* struck a mine in the sea between the islands of Kos and Kalymnos. The explosion tore the ship apart, and it sank in just two minutes. The vessel had a crew of about 200 men, plus another 200 soldiers—including a company from the British regiment known as the Buffs. Only about 100 people survived. They were rescued by an RAF air-sea rescue launch sent from Leros and by boats from the *Petard*, after spending more than three hours in the water.

Brigadier-General Davy was among the survivors. He later became Commander of Land Forces in the Adriatic region and retired from the army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it happened.

CuratorialText_x: image: An abstract composition of coiled and twisted girders of all sizes. This drawing explores the distortions made by bombs to building structures. The violence of the blast is evident in the rhythm and the form of the girders. Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol and returned there during the Second World War after over 10 years as an artist in Paris. She never gained an official war artist commission due to her left wing associations and the abstract nature of her work. However with the help of Bristol Museum and Art Gallery she gained a permit to draw the ruins in Bristol.

This was because of her left-wing political views and her abstract style. But with help from Bristol Museum and Art Gallery, she got a permit to draw the city's ruined buildings.

persona3_adapted: This drawing shows a tangle of twisted metal beams, all different sizes, coiled together in an abstract way. It captures the damage bombs caused to buildings during the war. Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol and returned there during the Second World War, after spending more than a decade working as an artist in Paris. CuratorialText_x: Acknowledged as one of the greatest landscape artists of all time, Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) knew Oxford extremely well. From childhood, he made numerous drawings and watercolours of the streets and colleges and the picturesque views surrounding the city. His celebrated oil painting of the High Street, Oxford is on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To mark the 250th anniversary of J.M.W. Turner's birth on 23 April 1775, explore this selection of his depictions of Oxford from the Ashmolean's collections. The Museum's Western Art Print Room houses just over 100 watercolours and drawings by Turner, as well as many landscape etchings. There are lively studies of animals, birds and fish, as well as evocative views of places that the artist visited on his travels in Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.

CuratorialText_x: In the second millennium BCE the island of Crete was home to a complex pre-Greek society known as ‘the Minoans’. When archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans started to excavate at the site of Knossos on Crete, in 1900, he frequently connected the things he found with ancient Cretan myths. He named the building at Knossos the ‘Palace of Minos’, after the mythical king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth was an ingenious maze commissioned by King Minos to imprison the ferocious Minotaur, a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the myth, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, the famous semi-divine hero. Following Theseus's victory, he returned to Greece, and became king and the founder of Athens. The Cretan myths originated in stories which the Greek and Roman inhabitants of the city of Knossos would have told long after the Palace had been destroyed.

CuratorialText_x:
Landscape painting has a long tradition in East Asian art. However, landscape did not become an important feature of Japanese woodblock prints until the early 1800s. By this time, Japanese citizens were taking part in more outdoor sightseeing activities, such as temple visits and boat trips, and there was a growing demand for souvenir images. In the competitive world of the publishing industry, publishers were constantly trying to develop new products; landscape prints were an appealing novelty.

Technical factors also played an important part in the emergence of the landscape print. Particularly significant was the introduction of the new imported pigment Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded very quickly, this brilliant colour gave artists more freedom of expression, especially in the depiction of sky and water

Here we feature a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige, as well as selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.

NameOfTheObject: View of the High Street, Oxford
CuratorialText_x: The painting resulted from an important early commission for two watercolours to be engraved for the annual calendar published by the University Press, the Oxford Almanack. They were so successful that Tuner eventually completed ten watercolours between 1798 and 1804. This work and one other were later painted in oils for the local print-seller and frame-maker, James Wyatt. Turner consulted Wyatt on the details of the architecture included in the view. He also introduced figures, members of the University and clergy, and some women ‘for the sake of colour’. The painting is one of the most fully documented of all Turner’s works because Wyatt kept his correspondence with the artist which was included in his posthumous sale. The original purpose of the commission was to have the design engraved. Wyatt settled on an oil painting, instead of a watercolour, half the size of Turner’s normal canvases, at a cost of 100 guineas. The painting was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1812. When, in the 1830s, Turner was choosing views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolours, he rejected the High Street. He felt that in this painting he had achieved an unparalleled view of technical mastery that he could not repeat.

This painting started as an important early job. Turner was asked to make two watercolors. They would be turned into engravings for a yearly calendar called the *Oxford Almanack*. The calendar was published by the University Press.

The watercolors were so successful that Turner made ten of them between 1798 and 1804. Later, he painted this work and one other in oils. He did this for a local print-seller and frame-maker named James Wyatt.

Turner asked Wyatt for advice on the details of the buildings in the scene. He also added people to the painting. These included members of the university, clergy, and some women. Turner said he added the women "for the sake of color."

This painting is one of Turner's best-documented works. That is because Wyatt kept the letters Turner sent him. Those letters were sold after Wyatt died.

The original goal of the job was to make an engraving from the design. But Wyatt decided to buy an oil painting instead of a watercolor. It was half the size of Turner's usual canvases. The cost was 100 guineas.

The painting was shown at the Royal Academy in 1812. In the 1830s, Turner chose views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolors. He did not pick the High Street. He felt that in this painting, he had reached a level of technical skill he could not match again.

The watercolours were so successful that Turner eventually completed ten of them between 1798 and 1804.

This work, along with one other, was later painted in oils. The commission came from James Wyatt, a local print-seller and frame-maker. Turner consulted Wyatt about the architectural details shown in the view. He also introduced figures into the composition: members of the University, clergy, and some women—the latter included “for the sake of colour.”

This painting is one of the most fully documented of all Turner’s works. The reason is that Wyatt kept his correspondence with the artist. This correspondence was later included in Wyatt’s posthumous sale.

The original purpose of the commission was to have the design engraved. However, Wyatt decided on an oil painting instead of a watercolour. The canvas was half the size of Turner’s usual works, and the cost was 100 guineas. The painting was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1812.

In the 1830s, Turner was selecting views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolours. He chose not to include the High Street. He felt that in this painting, he had achieved a level of technical mastery that was unparalleled—and that he could not repeat.
persona3_adapted:
This painting came from an early, important job: Turner was asked to create two watercolors that would be turned into engravings—printed images—for the *Oxford Almanack*, a yearly calendar published by the University Press. The works were so well received that Turner eventually completed ten watercolors for the project between 1798 and 1804. Later, this piece and one other were painted in oils (a type of paint) for a local seller of prints and picture frames named James Wyatt.

Turner consulted Wyatt about the architectural details shown in the scene. He also added figures—members of the university and clergy—along with some women, whom he included “for the sake of colour,” meaning to add visual interest and variety. This painting is one of the best-documented works in Turner’s career, because Wyatt kept the letters Turner sent him, and those letters were later sold after Wyatt’s death.

The original goal of the commission was to create a design that could be engraved. Wyatt decided on an oil painting instead of a watercolor, half the size of Turner’s usual canvases, for a fee of 100 guineas (a significant sum at the time). The painting was shown at the Royal Academy in 1812. Later, in the 1830s, when Turner was selecting views of Oxford for his most famous series of watercolors, he chose not to paint the High Street again. He felt that in this earlier work, he had achieved a level of technical skill that he could not surpass.

CuratorialText_x: As a nocturnal landscape and as a brilliantly structured composition, The Hunt in the Forest is a highly original painting. Hunting was an aristocratic pastime with its own rituals, but the idea of a hunt at night is playful and may be symbolic rather than realistic. In preparing this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first mapped out a grid on the panel's surface, fixing a central vanishing point to draw our gaze deep into the composition. In painting the subject, he incorporated details including the huntsmen's spears, the cut branches, logs and the water in the foreground as devices to bring focus to this space and to the receding forms of men and animals. Gold flecks in the foliage of the trees would have complemented the bright colours of the figures against the dark forest, and would have glistened and become animated when illuminated by candlelight. Paolo di Dono (1397-1475) was celebrated in his lifetime as a master of perspective, animals and landscape; his nickname, Uccello (‘Bird’), alludes to his depictions of the natural world. He was a versatile designer, working at times on mosaic and stained-glass commissions and all of these skills have been cleverly combined to create this masterpiece. The Hunt is what is known as a spalliera painting, which would have been mounted at shoulder height – whether as a backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden panelling of a room. The original client is unknown, but the painting was clearly intended for a Renaissance palace, perhaps in Urbino where Uccello worked for a time from 1465, or in Florence about 1470.

CuratorialText_x: A free copy, dating from the late 17th or early 18th century, of the fresco in one of the spandrels of the painted Loggia di Psiche in the Villa Farnesina, Rome. Raphael and his studio decorated this space in 1518-19 with scenes from the story of Cupid and Psyche. The spandrel from which WA1859.1 was copied may have been painted by Raphael's senior assistant, Giulio Romano. The copyist has altered the format to a square shape.

NameOfTheObject: Still Life with a Faience Figure
CuratorialText_x: Three of the objects in this still life are found elsewhere in Renoir’s late work: the silver beaker appears alongside a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life said to have been painted at Cagnes in 1910 and again in a similar composition without the knife, also said to have been painted in 1910; the bronze-coloured child on the column was included in a small study, dated c.1905 by the Daubervilles. The faience double salt, a type produced in quantity in the nineteenth century at Desvres in the Pas-de-Calais and at Quimper in Brittany, appears in at least four other sketches. A disembodied image of the same figure, seen from the back, hovers in upper right, probably a reflection in a mirror. Renoir, who worked as a painter at a ceramic manufactory in his early days, seems to have owned a number of pieces of faience of this popular type⁵ and used a variety of coloured and decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.
